statement: In anatomical position:

options.a: The body is lying down position with face directed upward
options.b: The body is erect with palm directed backward
options.c: The body erect, with arms on side, palms facing forward

explanation: In this position the body is regarded as standing erect, eyes looking toward horizon, upper limbs hanging by side with the palms of hands directed forward and feet are together with toes pointing forward.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which body plane divides body in equal 2 right & left halves:
explanation: Any plane parallel to the median plane is called a sagittal plane.
subject: Anatomy

statement: All are principal types of tissues except:
explanation: There are four types of principal tissues i.e. sepithelium, nervous, muscular, connective tissues.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding different positions of the body supine position is:

options.a: The person is lying on his face
options.d: is a person lying on his back

explanation: Supine is positioned with a flat lying on the back and face and chest facing upwards.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Rotates the radius medially so that the palm of the hand faces posteriorly and its dorsum faces anteriorly:
explanation: Pronation turns the dorsum of the hand forward without the rotation of the upper arm.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Movement by which the pad of the 1st digit (thumb) is brought to another digit pad:
explanation: During opposition, the thumb pad is brought to touch and oppose the finger pad of the extended fingers.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Anterolateral movement of scapula is called:
explanation: A forward movement of shoulder girdle and lower jaw is called protraction.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Spleen rests on:
explanation: The phrenicocolic ligament (also known as the sustentaculum lienis) is a fold of peritoneum extending from the splenic flexure of the colon to the diaphragm. It acts as a structural "shelf" or floor that supports the weight of the spleen in the upright position.
subject: Anatomy

statement: What is the anatomical location of spleen:
explanation: The spleen is located in the left hypochondrium, tucked deep under the protection of the diaphragm. It is entirely covered by the rib cage (specifically ribs 9 through 11), making it non-palpable in a healthy individual unless it is significantly enlarged.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The left rib that lies parallel to the long axis of spleen:
explanation: The long axis of the spleen lies parallel to the 10th rib. This orientation is clinically significant because as the spleen enlarges (splenomegaly), it typically grows along this axis toward the right iliac fossa, crossing the midline of the abdomen.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Spleen is supplied by splenic artery which is a branch of:
explanation: The splenic artery is the largest and most tortuous branch of the celiac trunk. It travels along the superior border of the pancreas, eventually entering the lienorenal ligament to reach the splenic hilum, where it divides into several segmental branches.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following ligaments connects the spleen to the left kidney:
explanation: The lienorenal (or splenorenal) ligament is a double fold of peritoneum that connects the hilum of the spleen to the left kidney. It is a vital surgical landmark because it serves as the "highway" for the splenic vessels and the tail of the pancreas.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following structures is located anterior to the spleen:
explanation: The stomach is the primary structure located anterior to the spleen. The visceral surface of the spleen actually bears a "gastric impression" where it rests against the fundus and the greater curvature of the stomach.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is a content of splenorenal ligament:
explanation: The splenic artery and vein are the most critical contents of the splenorenal ligament. Surgeons must be extremely careful when clamping this ligament during a splenectomy to avoid accidentally damaging the tail of the pancreas, which also sits within these folds.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Posteriorly, spleen is related to which ribs:
explanation: Posteriorly, the spleen is related to the 9th, 10th, and 11th ribs on the left side. It is separated from them only by the diaphragm and the costodiaphragmatic recess of the pleura, which explains why fractured ribs in this area often result in splenic laceration.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Oesophageal varices are caused by which one of the following:

options.d: Enlarged liver pressing on portal vein

explanation: Portal hypertension causes blood to back up into portosystemic anastomoses. In the lower esophagus, the high-pressure portal blood diverts into the submucosal veins (left gastric vein to esophageal veins), causing them to dilate into oesophageal varices that are prone to life-threatening rupture.
subject: Anatomy

statement: A 25 year old young man presented in emergency with blunt trauma abdomen. He is in shock with blood pressure 70/40; pulse 124/min. On examination, abdomen is tense & tender more on left upper abdomen. Which solid organ is most likely to be injured:
explanation: In cases of blunt trauma to the left upper abdomen, the spleen is the most likely solid organ to rupture. Because its "red pulp" is essentially a blood-filled sponge enclosed in a thin capsule, injury leads to massive internal bleeding, rapid onset of shock, and abdominal tenderness.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which one of the following is a long bone:
explanation: Miniature long bones are types of long bones and include metacarpal, metatarsal and phalanges.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The characteristic of elastic cartilage includes all of the following except:

options.a: It does not ordinarily undergo calcification
options.b: Its matrix is abundant in elastic fibers
options.d: it is found in pinna, tip of nose, and in pharyngo-tympanic tube

explanation: It is found in structures which undergo functional distortion and contain a lot of elastic fibers.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Patella is a type of:
explanation: Patella is the largest sesamoid bone of the body. It is embedded in the tendon of quadriceps femoris. Sesamoid bone is a bony nodule within tendon or muscle.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding bone, choose correct statement:

options.c: Pneumatic bones contain large fluid space

explanation: Sesamoid bone is found in tendon and muscle and subjected to friction. Thus, they are covered by cartilage or fibrous tissue hence have no periosteum.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Primary ossification center:

options.c: Responsible for increasing length of bone

explanation: During bone development, the shaft (diaphysis) develops from the primary ossification center whereas epiphysis are formed from secondary ossification center.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Periosteal arteries are derived from:

options.a: A major artery passing near the bone
options.c: Muscular arteries of muscle attached to joint

explanation: The periosteal arteries are derived from arteries of those muscles which are attached to the bone. In addition to providing nutrition of the periosteum, the periosteal arteries aid in the blood supply of the outer part of cortex.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Knuckle shaped articular surface of bone is called:
explanation: A rounded protuberance at the end of a bone is called Condyle. They usually occur in pairs i.e. medial and lateral Condyle of femur.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a hyaline cartilage:
explanation: Hyaline cartilage is glossy in appearance. It is found in the skeleton of embryo, larynx, trachea and bronchi. It is also found in the external part of the nose.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of following is not a fibrocartilage:
explanation: Fibrocartilage is resilient and pliable. It is found in intervertebral disc, disc of pubic symphysis, labra of some joint sockets and meniscus of the knee.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not an elastic cartilage:
explanation: Elastic cartilage has special characteristics of being flexible and elastic. It is found in structures that undergo functional distortion like pinna of ear, Eustachian tube, Epiglottis. The external part of the nose is hyaline in nature.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Choose the incorrect match:
explanation: Atavistic Epiphysis represent those bones which were phylogenetically independent but have become fused with other bones in humans. For example, Atavistic Epiphysis is the coracoid process of scapula.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Wormian bone is found in suture:
explanation: Accessory bones commonly occur in the cranium as small bony islands within the sutural joints between the bones of the skull such bones are called Wormian bones, most commonly found in lambdoid suture.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Sesamoid bone is not found in:
explanation: Sesamoid bone is a bony nodule embedded within a tendon or muscle. They act as pulleys, providing a smooth surface for tendons to slide over. They are found in tendons of flexor pollicis brevis, adductor pollicis, flexor hallucis brevis.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Riders Bone is formed in:
explanation: Accessory bones that develop in muscle or other soft tissue such bones are called heterotrophic bones, example riders bone that develop in adductor muscles of the thigh.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Riders Bone is an example of:
explanation: Accessory bones that develop in muscle or other soft tissue such bones are called heterotrophic bones, example riders bone that develop in adductor muscles of the thigh.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The part of a long bone that is responsible for longitudinal growth during development is:
explanation: The epiphyseal plate (growth plate) is a plate of hyaline cartilage located between the epiphysis (end of the bone) and the metaphysis of a long bone. It is responsible for longitudinal growth (increase in length) of long bones during childhood and adolescence.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which type of bone is characterized by a network of trabeculae arranged along lines of stress:
explanation: Cancellous bone (also called spongy or trabecular bone) is characterized by a mesh-like network of thin bony plates called trabeculae. These trabeculae are arranged along the lines of stress or mechanical load placed on the bone. This arrangement provides maximum strength with minimal weight.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The nutrient artery enters the shaft of a long bone through the:
explanation: The nutrient artery is the main blood supply of a long bone. It enters the shaft (diaphysis) of the long bone through a small opening called the nutrient foramen. After entering the nutrient foramen, the artery passes through the nutrient canal.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The structural unit of compact bone is known as:
explanation: The osteon, also called the Haversian system, is the basic structural and functional unit of compact bone. Compact bone is organized into many cylindrical units called osteons that run parallel to the long axis of the bone.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which cells are primarily responsible for bone resorption:
explanation: Osteoclasts are the cells responsible for bone resorption (breakdown of bone tissue). They are large, multinucleated cells derived from the monocyte–macrophage lineage.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The periosteum is firmly attached to bone by:
explanation: The periosteum is firmly attached to the underlying bone by thick bundles of collagen fibers called Sharpey’s fibers.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which type of ossification is primarily responsible for the formation of flat bones of the skull:
explanation: Intramembranous ossification is the process by which bone develops directly from mesenchymal connective tissue without a cartilage model. This type of ossification forms the flat bones of the skull,
subject: Anatomy

statement: The metaphysis of a long bone is best described as the:

options.c: Flared part between diaphysis and epiphysis

explanation: The metaphysis is the expanded (flared) region of a long bone located between the diaphysis (shaft) and the epiphysis (end). It contains the epiphyseal plate (growth plate) in growing bones. This region is highly vascular and metabolically active.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following bones is classified as a sesamoid bone:
explanation: A sesamoid bone is a small, round bone that develops within a tendon where it passes over a joint. The patella (kneecap) is the largest sesamoid bone in the human body. It is embedded within the tendon of the quadriceps femoris muscle.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Volkmann’s canals in compact bone primarily serve to:
explanation: Volkmann’s canals (perforating canals) are transverse or oblique channels that connect adjacent Haversian canals with each other, link the periosteum and endosteum to the Haversian canals, and allow blood vessels and nerves to pass across the bone.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following structures contains fibrocartilage without a perichondrium and primarily resists compressive forces: 27.
explanation: The intervertebral disc is composed mainly of fibrocartilage, which is a type of cartilage that does not have a perichondrium. It is located between adjacent vertebrae
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which cartilage type is rich in elastic fibers and is essential for maintaining the shape of structures that must remain flexible:
explanation: Elastic cartilage is a type of cartilage that contains abundant elastic fibers in its matrix. These elastic fibers make the cartilage highly flexible while maintaining its shape.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following joints contains articular cartilage that lacks a perichondrium and is nourished by synovial fluid:
explanation: In synovial joints, the ends of bones are covered by articular cartilage, which is a specialized hyaline cartilage.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following structures contains fibrocartilage that functions primarily to absorb shock and resist tensile stress:
explanation: The annulus fibrosus is the outer ring of the intervertebral disc and is made of fibrocartilage. Fibrocartilage contains dense bundles of type I collagen fibers. It is highly resistant to tensile (pulling) forces. It works together with the gel-like nucleus pulposus to absorb compressive shock in the spine.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Elastic cartilage is typically found in which of the following structures that require repeated bending without loss of shape:
explanation: Elastic cartilage is a type of cartilage that contains abundant elastic fibers in the matrix, which gives it flexibility and resilience. It is specifically designed to allow repeated bending without losing shape. It is covered by perichondrium, which helps in nutrient supply and repair.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following structures contains hyaline cartilage that later undergoes endochondral ossification during development:
explanation: The epiphyseal plate (growth plate) is composed of hyaline cartilage. During childhood and adolescence, this cartilage is the site of longitudinal bone growth.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is a secondary cartilaginous joint composed of fibrocartilage:
explanation: The pubic symphysis is a secondary cartilaginous joint composed of fibrocartilage. It is slightly movable and functions to absorb shock between the two pubic bones during movement, providing stability while allowing minor flexibility.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The cartilage forming the tracheal rings is best described as:
explanation: The tracheal rings are made of hyaline cartilage, which forms rigid C-shaped structures that maintain airway patency. Hyaline cartilage is strong enough to resist compression, yet slightly flexible to accommodate movement of the neck and thorax.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following structures contains elastic cartilage providing resilience and recoil:
explanation: The epiglottis contains elastic cartilage, which is rich in elastic fibers. This allows it to bend repeatedly during swallowing and speech and return to its original shape, providing both resilience and flexibility.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which cartilage type forms the majority of the fetal skeleton before ossification occurs:
explanation: The majority of the fetal skeleton is initially composed of hyaline cartilage, which serves as a template for endochondral ossification. This cartilage gradually ossifies to form most long bones, allowing for proper skeletal growth and development.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a rotator cuff muscle:
explanation: Teres Major: The rotator cuff consists of the SITS muscles (Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis) which fuse with the joint capsule to provide stability. Teres major is an extrinsic muscle that adducts and medially rotates the humerus but does not contribute to the "cuff" mechanism.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The medial wall of the anatomical snuff box consists of the tendon from:
explanation: Extensor Pollicis Longus: The anatomical snuffbox is a triangular depression where the medial (ulnar) boundary is formed by the tendon of the Extensor Pollicis Longus (EPL). The lateral boundary is formed by the Abductor Pollicis Longus and Extensor Pollicis Brevis.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following group of lymph nodes is not present in the axilla:
explanation: Cervical Group: The axilla contains five specific lymph node groups: Pectoral, Subscapular, Lateral, Central, and Apical. Cervical nodes are located in the neck; while they communicate with the apical group via the lymphatic chain, they are not anatomically part of the axillary group.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The muscle attached to the coracoid process of scapula is:
explanation: Short head of biceps: The coracoid process serves as the "triple attachment" site for the Pectoralis minor, Coracobrachialis, and the Short head of Biceps Brachii. The long head of the biceps is excluded as it originates from the supraglenoid tubercle of the scapula.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The supraspinatus muscle will:

options.c: Extend and laterally rotate the arm
options.d: Extend and medially rotate the arm

explanation: Abduct the arm: The Supraspinatus is the primary initiator of shoulder abduction, responsible for the first 0-15° of movement before the deltoid takes over. It acts by pulling the humeral head medially and downwards into the glenoid cavity.
subject: Anatomy

statement: This nerve arises from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus:
explanation: Axillary nerve: Using the STAR mnemonic for the posterior cord (Subscapular, Thoracodorsal, Axillary, Radial), the axillary nerve (C5, C6) emerges to supply the deltoid and teres minor. The other options arise from the roots or the lateral/medial cords.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The nerve that innervates the trapezius muscle is:
explanation: Spinal accessory nerve: The trapezius is unique because its motor supply comes from the Cranial Nerve XI (Spinal Accessory), while its sensory/proprioceptive fibers come from the C3 and C4 spinal nerves. It is the only muscle listed not supplied by the brachial plexus.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Identify the artery that does not supply the breast:
explanation: Brachial artery: The breast is supplied by the Internal Thoracic, Lateral Thoracic, and Posterior Intercostal arteries. The brachial artery begins at the lower border of the teres major, which is distal to the thoracic region and the breast's anatomical location.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a branch of the brachial artery:
explanation: Common interosseous artery: The brachial artery provides the Profunda Brachii and the Ulnar Collateral arteries before dividing at the elbow. The common interosseous artery is a branch of the ulnar artery, arising in the proximal forearm rather than the arm.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following does not belong to the superficial group of flexor compartment of the forearm:
explanation: Flexor pollicis longus: The superficial flexor group consists of the Pronator Teres, FCR, Palmaris Longus, and FCU. The Flexor Pollicis Longus (FPL) is situated in the deep layer of the anterior compartment alongside the Flexor Digitorum Profundus.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The pectoralis major muscle:

options.a: Is innervated by the thoracodorsal nerve.
options.b: Is an abductor of the arm.
options.c: Originates from the lateral third of the clavicle.
options.d: Is a powerful flexor of the forearm.
options.e: Is an adductor of the arm.

explanation: Adductor of the arm: The Pectoralis major is a large, fan-shaped muscle that primarily acts as a powerful adductor and medial rotator of the humerus. It also assists in flexion (clavicular head) and extension from a flexed position (sternocostal head), but it does not abduct.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The cubital fossa:

options.b: Has the triceps brachii tendon coursing through it.
options.c: Is bounded laterally by the pronator teres
options.e: Is a quadrangular space in front of the elbow.

explanation: Contains the median nerve: The cubital fossa is a triangular space containing (from medial to lateral) the median nerve, brachial artery, biceps tendon, and the radial nerve (deep). It is bounded medially by the pronator teres and laterally by the brachioradialis.
subject: Anatomy

statement: All of the following structures are located at the proximal end of the humerus except:
explanation: Olecranon process: The proximal humerus includes the head, anatomical/surgical necks, and the greater/lesser tubercles. The olecranon process is the prominent bony projection of the proximal ulna that forms the point of the elbow, not part of the humerus.
subject: Anatomy

statement: This nerve innervates the trapezius:
explanation: Spinal accessory nerve: Motor innervation to the trapezius is provided by the Spinal Accessory Nerve (CN XI), while sensory fibers for proprioception arise from the C3 and C4 spinal nerves. It is not supplied by the branches of the brachial plexus.
subject: Anatomy

statement: This nerve arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus:
explanation: Median nerve: The median nerve is formed by two roots: the lateral root (lateral cord) and the medial root (medial cord). While the ulnar nerve is the most famous medial cord branch, the median nerve is the correct choice here as it relies on that medial cord contribution.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The structure that holds the head of ulna against the radius is the:
explanation: Annular ligament: The annular ligament forms a collar around the head of the radius, holding it securely in the radial notch of the ulna. This mechanism allows the radius to rotate during pronation and supination while maintaining joint stability.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following statements about the glenohumeral joint is true:

options.a: The glenoid fossa is larger than the humeral head
options.b: The tendon of the subscapularis muscle reinforces the posterior side of the joint
options.c: The glenohumeral ligament strongly support the joint
options.d: The supraspinatus tendon crosses superior to the joint capsule
options.e: The tendon of the short head of the biceps brachii muscle passes through the joint

explanation: Supraspinatus tendon crosses superior to the joint: The supraspinatus tendon passes under the acromion and superiorly to the joint capsule to insert on the greater tubercle. Note that the humeral head is actually much larger than the glenoid fossa, necessitating the glenoid labrum for depth.
subject: Anatomy

statement: All the following statements are true except:

options.a: Shoulder joint is a synovial joint
options.b: Brachial plexus is formed by the ventral rami of the C5-T1
options.c: Axillary vein is the continuation of the brachial vein
options.d: Median cubital vein shunts blood from the cephalic vein into the basilic vein
options.e: Mammary gland is a modified sweat gland

explanation: Axillary vein is the continuation of the brachial vein: This is false because the axillary vein is primarily the continuation of the basilic vein at the lower border of the teres major muscle. It does receive the brachial veins, but the basilic is the main "root".
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following are the branches of the brachial artery except:
explanation: Common interosseous artery: The brachial artery provides the profunda brachii and ulnar collateral branches. The common interosseous artery is a branch of the ulnar artery, arising just distal to the bifurcation of the brachial artery in the forearm.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Quadrangular space is bounded by the following structures except:
explanation: Infraspinatus: The quadrangular space is bounded by the Teres minor/Subscapularis (superior), Teres major (inferior), long head of triceps (medial), and the surgical neck of the humerus (lateral). The Infraspinatus is located superior to the Teres minor and does not form a boundary of this specific neurovascular space.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following statements are true about the mammary gland except:

options.b: It is a modified sebaceous gland
options.c: Lymphatic drainage is to the axillary lymph nodes
options.e: Ligaments of cooper anchor it to pectoral fascia

explanation: Modified sebaceous gland: The mammary gland is anatomically a modified sweat gland (specifically an apocrine gland), not a sebaceous gland. It consists of 15–20 lobes and is anchored to the skin/fascia by the suspensory ligaments of Cooper.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following tenders are inserted into the base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb except:
explanation: Abductor pollicis longus: The Abductor Pollicis Longus (APL) inserts into the base of the 1st metacarpal bone. In contrast, the Extensor Pollicis Brevis (EPB) and Flexor Pollicis Brevis (FPB) insert into the base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Concerning the female breast all of the following statements are true except:

options.a: It is firmly attached to the pectoralis major muscle.
options.b: There is a subareolar lymph plexus.
options.c: Axillary lymph nodes receive more than half of the lymph of the breast.
options.d: Lymph from the lateral part of the drain parasternal lymph nodes.
options.e: 90 % of lymph may pass to posterior intercostals nodes.

explanation: Firmly attached to the pectoralis major: The breast is separated from the pectoralis major muscle by the retromammary space, a layer of loose areolar tissue. This space allows the breast a degree of independent movement; "firm attachment" usually indicates pathological invasion, such as carcinoma.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following proximal row of carpal bones except:
explanation: Trapezium: The proximal row of carpals consists of the Scaphoid, Lunate, Triquetrum, and Pisiform ("She Looks Too Pretty"). The Trapezium belongs to the distal row ("Try To Catch Her") and articulates with the first metacarpal.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The tendons of the following muscles form the rotator cuff except:
explanation: Teres major: The rotator cuff "SITS" muscles include the Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, and Subscapularis. The Teres major is considered an "extrinsic" shoulder muscle and does not fuse with the joint capsule to form the cuff.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The lymphatic drainage of the breast involve all of the following except:
explanation: Inguinal Lymph Nodes: Lymph from the breast drains primarily to the axillary nodes (75%), with the remainder going to parasternal, abdominal, and posterior intercostal nodes. Inguinal nodes drain the lower limbs and perineum and have no anatomical connection to the breast.
subject: Anatomy

statement: All of the following bones develop through ossification except:
explanation: Clavicle: While most long bones develop through endochondral ossification (replacing a cartilage model), the clavicle is unique. It is the first bone in the body to ossify and does so primarily through intramembranous ossification.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding shoulder joint all are true except:

options.a: Rotator cuff muscles are attached to capsule which is deficient inferiorly.
options.c: Subacromial bursa communicates to shoulder joint.
options.d: Nerve to serratus anterior root of brachial plexus.
options.e: Subscapular nerve arises from post cord

explanation: Subacromial bursa communicates to shoulder joint: Normally, the subacromial bursa is separated from the joint cavity by the rotator cuff tendons (specifically supraspinatus). It only communicates with the joint if there is a pathological tear in the rotator cuff.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The radial nerve:

options.a: Supplies sensation to medial side of dorsum of hand
options.b: Arises from upper and middle trunks of brachial plexus
options.c: May be damaged by fractures of neck of humerus

explanation: Supplies extensor muscles: The radial nerve is the nerve of the extensor compartment; it supplies the triceps in the arm and all the muscles of the posterior compartment of the forearm (extensors and supinator). It arises from the posterior cord.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following statements are true about the mammary gland except:

options.b: It is a modified sebaceous gland
options.c: Lymphatic drainage is to the axillary lymph nodes
options.e: Ligaments of cooper anchor it to pectoral fascia

explanation: Modified sebaceous gland: As established previously, the mammary gland is an apocrine sweat gland (modified for milk production). It is not a sebaceous (oil-producing) gland.
subject: Anatomy

statement: This nerve is subject to injury as a result of fractures at the mid-shaft of the humerus:
explanation: Radial nerve: The radial nerve lies in the spiral (radial) groove directly against the mid-shaft of the humerus. A fracture here frequently results in radial nerve palsy, leading to "wrist drop" due to loss of extensor function.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The medial wall of the anatomical snuff box consists of the tendon from:
explanation: Extensor Pollicis Longus: The medial (ulnar) wall is formed by the tendon of the Extensor Pollicis Longus. The lateral (radial) wall is formed by the tendons of the Abductor Pollicis Longus and Extensor Pollicis Brevis.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The lymphatic drainage of the breast involves all of the following except:
explanation: Inguinal Lymph Nodes: Breast lymph drains toward the axilla, sternum, or abdomen. Inguinal nodes are located in the groin and drain the lower limbs and lower abdominal wall, having no role in breast drainage.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Injury to ulnar nerve results in absence of sensation in which part of the hand:
explanation: Little Finger (pinkie): The ulnar nerve provides sensory innervation to the medial one and a half fingers (little finger and the medial half of the ring finger). Loss of sensation in the little finger is a hallmark of ulnar nerve injury.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Following are posterior to the flexor retinaculum of the wrist except:
explanation: Ulnar nerve: The carpal tunnel (posterior to the flexor retinaculum) contains the median nerve and 9 tendons (FDS, FDP, FPL). The ulnar nerve and artery pass anterior to the retinaculum through the Canal of Guyon.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Coracoid process of scapula gives attachment to:
explanation: Short head of biceps: The coracoid process provides origin for the short head of biceps and coracobrachialis, and insertion for the pectoralis minor. The long head of the biceps originates from the supraglenoid tubercle.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The forearm remains in supination because of loss of function of this nerve:
explanation: Median nerve: The median nerve supplies the pronator teres and pronator quadratus. If these are paralyzed, the hand remains in a supinated position because the supinators (radial and musculocutaneous nerves) are unopposed.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The action of the supraspinatus muscle is to:

options.d: Extend and laterally rotate the arm
options.e: Extend and medially rotate the arm

explanation: Abduct the arm: The supraspinatus muscle initiates abduction of the arm at the shoulder joint (first 0–15°). It also stabilizes the humeral head against the glenoid fossa during deltoid-mediated abduction.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The structure that holds the head of ulna against the radius is the:
explanation: Annular ligament: The annular ligament encircles the head of the radius (keeping it against the radial notch of the ulna). Note: The question likely meant radial head; at the distal joint/ulnar head, the articular disc provides this stability.
subject: Anatomy

statement: This nerve arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus:
explanation: Median nerve: The median nerve is formed by a lateral root and a medial root; the latter is a direct branch of the medial cord (C8, T1). Other medial cord branches include the ulnar and medial pectoral nerves.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following nerves innervates the trapezius:
explanation: Spinal accessory nerve: The trapezius receives its motor supply from the Spinal Accessory Nerve (CN XI). It is the only muscle listed that does not receive its primary motor innervation from the brachial plexus.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Muscles attached to the lateral third of the clavicle are deltoid and the:
explanation: Trapezius: The lateral third of the clavicle serves as the attachment point for the deltoid (anteriorly) and the trapezius (posteriorly). These muscles help suspend the upper limb from the axial skeleton.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding joints, which of the following joint is less stable but more mobile:
explanation: Shoulder joint: The shoulder is a "ball and socket" joint where a large humeral head fits into a shallow glenoid cavity. This geometry allows for the greatest range of motion but makes it the most unstable joint.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding shoulder joint, the rotator cuff support is weaker:
explanation: In inferior direction: The rotator cuff (SITS) supports the joint superiorly, anteriorly, and posteriorly. There is no muscular support inferiorly, which is why most shoulder dislocations occur in an anteroinferior direction.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The lateral boundary of cubital fossa is formed by:
explanation: Brachioradialis: The cubital fossa is bounded laterally by the brachioradialis and medially by the pronator teres. Its roof is formed by skin and fascia, while the floor is the brachialis and supinator.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding nerve supply of muscle, the opponens pollicis is supplied by:

options.d: Superficial branch of the radial nerve
options.e: A branch of the median nerve

explanation: A branch of the median nerve: The recurrent branch of the median nerve (C8, T1) supplies the thenar muscles: Lateral two lumbricals, Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, and Flexor pollicis brevis.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome is related to compression of:
explanation: The median nerve mainly: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome involves the compression of the median nerve as it passes through the narrow space between the carpal bones and the flexor retinaculum, causing pain and numbness.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The anatomic snuffbox is bounded laterally by:
explanation: Extensor pollicis brevis: The lateral (radial) boundary of the anatomical snuffbox is formed by the tendons of the Abductor Pollicis Longus and the Extensor Pollicis Brevis. The scaphoid and trapezium form the floor, while the Extensor Pollicis Longus forms the medial boundary.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The structure passing superficial to the flexor retinaculum is the:
explanation: Ulnar artery: Structures passing superficial to the flexor retinaculum include the ulnar nerve, ulnar artery, palmaris longus tendon, and the palmar cutaneous branches of the median and ulnar nerves. The contents of the carpal tunnel (median nerve and tendons) lie deep to it.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The deformity in Erb's paralysis is called:
explanation: The waiter's tip: Erb's paralysis (C5, C6 injury) results in a "waiter’s tip" deformity where the arm hangs by the side (loss of abductors), is medially rotated (loss of lateral rotators), and the forearm is extended and pronated (loss of biceps).
subject: Anatomy

statement: Muscle attached to medial lip of bicipital groove is:
explanation: Teres major: Remember the "Lady between two majors" mnemonic for the bicipital groove: Pectoralis major (lateral lip), Latissimus dorsi (floor), and Teres major (medial lip). Teres major acts to medially rotate and adduct the humerus at this attachment.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The surgical neck of the humerus is related to the:
explanation: Axillary nerve: The axillary nerve and the posterior circumflex humeral artery wrap directly around the surgical neck of the humerus. Fractures at this site are the most common cause of axillary nerve palsy and deltoid atrophy.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The medial part of flexor digitorum profundus is innervated by:
explanation: Ulnar nerve: The Flexor Digitorum Profundus has a dual nerve supply: the lateral half (supplying index and middle fingers) is innervated by the median nerve, while the medial half (supplying ring and little fingers) is innervated by the ulnar nerve.
subject: Anatomy

statement: All of the small muscles of the hand are supplied by the:
explanation: T1 root: The T1 spinal nerve root is the primary contributor to the intrinsic (small) muscles of the hand via the ulnar and median nerves. Damage to this root (e.g., Klumpke’s paralysis) leads to a total "claw hand" due to loss of all these muscles.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The inferior boundary quadrangular space is formed by the:
explanation: Teres major muscle: The quadrangular space is bounded superiorly by the teres minor, inferiorly by the teres major, medially by the long head of triceps, and laterally by the surgical neck of the humerus. It transmits the axillary nerve.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Injury of the anatomical snuff box produce massive bleeding due to damage of:
explanation: Radial artery: The radial artery passes deep to the tendons of the APL and EPB to lie in the floor of the anatomical snuffbox before passing between the heads of the first dorsal interosseous muscle. Injury to this area often leads to arterial bleeding.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following are the branches of the brachial artery except:
explanation: Common interosseous artery: The brachial artery gives off the profunda brachii, nutrient artery, and ulnar collaterals before bifurcating at the elbow. The common interosseous artery is a branch of the ulnar artery, arising in the proximal forearm .
subject: Anatomy

statement: Quadrangular space is bounded by the following structures except:
explanation: Infraspinatus: The quadrangular space is bounded by the Teres minor/Subscapularis (top), Teres major (bottom), long head of triceps (medial), and humerus (lateral). The Infraspinatus is located superior to the Teres minor and does not form part of this boundary.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following 'statements are true about the mammary gland except:

options.b: It is a modified sebaceous gland
options.c: Lymphatic drainage is to the axillary lymph nodes
options.e: Ligaments of cooper anchor it to pectoral fascia

explanation: Modified sebaceous gland: The mammary gland is anatomically a modified apocrine sweat gland. It is located within the superficial fascia and is supported by the suspensory ligaments of Cooper.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Structure piercing the clavipectoral fascia is:
explanation: Lateral pectoral nerve: The clavipectoral fascia is pierced by four structures (Mnemonic CALL): Cephalic vein, ThoracoAcromial artery, Lateral pectoral nerve, and Lymphatics. The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor first.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following bone articulates with scaphoid bone:
explanation: Radius: The radiocarpal (wrist) joint is formed by the distal end of the radius articulating with the scaphoid and lunate bones. The ulna is excluded from this joint by an articular disc.
subject: Anatomy

statement: A patient has developed winging of scapula, which of the following nerve is injured:
explanation: Long thoracic nerve: The long thoracic nerve (C5-C7) supplies the serratus anterior, which holds the medial border of the scapula against the rib cage. Paralysis of this nerve causes the scapula to protrude posteriorly, known as winging.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Cancer of breast is common in its upper outer quadrant. It spreads commonly to:
explanation: Axillary lymph nodes: Approximately 75% of the lymphatic drainage from the breast, especially from the upper outer quadrant, enters the axillary lymph nodes (primarily the pectoral/anterior group).
subject: Anatomy

statement: The spine of scapula is continued laterally as:
explanation: Acromion process: The spine of the scapula is a prominent plate of bone on the posterior surface that continues laterally as the flattened acromion process, which articulates with the clavicle at the AC joint.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Loss of flexion at elbow joint may be caused by injury to:
explanation: Musculocutaneous nerve: This nerve supplies the muscles of the anterior compartment of the arm: Coracobrachialis, Biceps brachii, and Brachialis. Injury leads to a total loss of primary elbow flexion and weakened supination.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The nerve most likely to be injured in mid shaft humeral fracture is:
explanation: Radial nerve: The radial nerve winds around the mid-shaft of the humerus in the spiral (radial) groove. Fractures at this site often cause nerve injury, resulting in "wrist drop" due to paralysis of the extensors.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Flexor retinaculum is attached laterally to:
explanation: Trapezium: The flexor retinaculum (roof of the carpal tunnel) is attached laterally to the tubercle of the scaphoid and the ridge of the trapezium. Medially, it attaches to the pisiform bone and the hook of the hamate.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Concerning the female breast, choose the correct answer:

options.a: It extends transversely from anterior to mid axillary line
options.b: Its bed is formed mainly by serratus anterior muscle
options.c: Its nipple has no fat, hair or sweat glands
options.e: Its lymphatics drain mainly in the parasternal lymph nodes

explanation: Its nipple has no fat, hair or sweat glands: The nipple is composed of sensitive erectile smooth muscle and is perforated by lactiferous ducts. While it contains modified sebaceous glands (Montgomery’s tubercles), it uniquely lacks fat, hair follicles, and sweat glands.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which muscles divides the axillary artery into 3 parts:
explanation: Pectoralis minor: The Pectoralis minor muscle crosses the axillary artery, logically dividing it into three parts: the first part (proximal), second part (posterior/deep), and third part (distal to the muscle).
subject: Anatomy

statement: The inter tubercular sulcus of the humerus contains:

options.a: Triceps tendon branch of posterior circumflex humeral artery
options.b: Triceps tendon & ascending branch of posterior circumflex humeral artery
options.c: Triceps tendon & biceps tendon descending branch of posterior circumflex humeral artery
options.d: Biceps tendon & descending branch of anterior circumflex humeral artery
options.e: Biceps tendon & ascending branch of anterior circumflex humeral artery

explanation: Biceps tendon & ascending branch of anterior circumflex humeral artery: The intertubercular (bicipital) sulcus lodges the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii (enclosed in a synovial sheath) and the ascending branch of the anterior circumflex humeral artery.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Injury of which nerve can lead to claw hand:
explanation: Ulnar nerve: The ulnar nerve (C8, T1) supplies most intrinsic hand muscles. Injury leads to Claw Hand (Main en Griffe), characterized by hyperextension of the metacarpophalangeal joints and flexion of the interphalangeal joints due to loss of lumbrical action.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which is the correct list of bone in the distal carpal row:
explanation: Trapezium, trapezoid, capitate & hamate: The carpal bones are arranged in two rows. The distal row (from lateral to medial) consists of the Trapezium, Trapezoid, Capitate, and Hamate—remembered by the mnemonic: "Trapezium Trapezoid Capitate Hamate".
subject: Anatomy

statement: Adduction of wrist joint is function of:

options.b: Extensor carpi ulnaris and flexor carpi ulnaris

explanation: Extensor carpi ulnaris and flexor carpi ulnaris: Adduction (ulnar deviation) is a synergistic movement produced by the simultaneous contraction of the Flexor Carpi Ulnaris and the Extensor Carpi Ulnaris, which cancel out each other's flexion/extension components.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The movement of supination and pronation occur at the:
explanation: Radio ulnar joint: Supination and pronation occur through the rotation of the radius around the ulna at the proximal and distal radioulnar joints. The elbow joint proper is strictly for flexion and extension.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which structure passes below the flexor retinaculum at the wrist:

options.b: Palmar cutaneous nerve of median nerve

explanation: Median nerve: The median nerve passes deep to the flexor retinaculum through the carpal tunnel. In contrast, the ulnar nerve, ulnar vessels, and palmaris longus tendon pass superficial to the retinaculum.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which artery accompanies the radial nerve into the posterior compartment of the arm:
explanation: Profunda brachii artery: The profunda brachii artery (deep artery of the arm) accompanies the radial nerve as it enters the posterior compartment to travel within the spiral (radial) groove of the humerus.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The apex of axilla is bounded by:
explanation: Lateral border of first rib: The apex of the axilla (cervicoaxillary canal) is a truncated passageway bounded anteriorly by the clavicle, posteriorly by the superior border of the scapula, and medially by the lateral border of the first rib.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding the clavicle, all are correct except:

options.a: It is an example of the short bones
options.b: It is the first bone to ossify in the fetal life
options.d: It is the commonly fractured bone

explanation: It is an example of the short bones: The clavicle is a long bone, despite its horizontal orientation and lack of a medullary cavity. It is unique for being the first bone to ossify (in membrane) and serves as a strut for the upper limb.
subject: Anatomy

statement: In difficult labour, undue separation of the neck from shoulder can cause injury to:
explanation: Upper trunk of brachial plexus: Excessive separation of the neck and shoulder (common in birth trauma or falls) stretches the upper trunk (C5, C6). This results in Erb-Duchenne paralysis, characterized by the "waiter's tip" limb position.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The force applied to the upper limb is transmitted to the trunk through:
explanation: Coraco-clavicular ligament: While the acromioclavicular joint connects the bones, the coracoclavicular ligament (conoid and trapezoid parts) is the primary structure that transmits the weight of the upper limb to the clavicle and subsequently the axial skeleton.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Stabilizer muscles of shoulder joint are following except:
explanation: Deltoid: The "SITS" muscles and the long head of the biceps act as dynamic stabilizers by pulling the humeral head into the glenoid. The deltoid is a prime mover (abductor); its vertical pull can actually destabilize the joint by pushing the humerus upward against the acromion.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Opponens pollicis muscle arise from:
explanation: Trapezium: The Opponens pollicis, a thenar muscle, originates from the flexor retinaculum and the tubercle of the trapezium. It inserts on the lateral border of the 1st metacarpal to rotate the thumb into opposition.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The tendon which muscle passes within the shoulder joint cavity:
explanation: Long head of bicep brachi: The tendon of the long head of the biceps is unique because it is intracapsular (traveling through the joint cavity) but remains extrasynovial. It originates from the supraglenoid tubercle of the scapula.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Damage to which of the following nerve will lead to loss of sensation over the deltoid muscle:
explanation: Axillary: The axillary nerve (C5, C6) gives off the upper lateral cutaneous nerve of the arm. This branch provides sensory innervation to the skin over the lower half of the deltoid muscle, known as the "regimental badge" area.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The back of the medial epicondyle of humerus is related to the:
explanation: Ulnar nerve: The ulnar nerve passes posterior to the medial epicondyle of the humerus in a groove (the ulnar groove). It is superficial here, making it highly susceptible to compression or "funny bone" injuries.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The thoracoacromial artery is a branch of the:

options.b: First part of the axillary artery
options.c: Second part of the axillary artery
options.d: Third part of the axillary artery

explanation: Second part of the axillary artery: The axillary artery is divided by the pectoralis minor. The second part (lying behind the muscle) gives off two branches: the thoracoacromial trunk and the lateral thoracic artery.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Lumbricals are responsible for:

options.a: Flexion at metacarpophalangeal and extension at interphalangeal joint
options.b: Extension at metacarpophalangeal joint and flexion at interphalangeal joint

explanation: Flexion at MCP and extension at IP joint: Lumbricals have a unique insertion into the extensor expansions. This position allows them to flex the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints while simultaneously extending the interphalangeal (IP) joints, a movement crucial for the "writing position".
subject: Anatomy

statement: Fractures at the mid-shaft of the humerus leads to the injury to:
explanation: Radial nerve: The radial nerve travels within the spiral groove on the posterior aspect of the humeral shaft. Mid-shaft fractures frequently cause nerve compression or laceration, resulting in the clinical sign of wrist drop.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Latissimus dorsi muscle:

options.b: Extends and laterally rotates the arm
options.c: Extends, and medially rotates the arm

explanation: Extends, and medially rotates the arm: Latissimus dorsi (the "swimmer's muscle") inserts into the floor of the bicipital groove. Its contraction produces powerful extension, adduction, and medial rotation of the humerus.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The bone developing intramembranous ossification is:
explanation: Clavicle: While most long bones develop from a cartilage model (endochondral), the clavicle is the first bone to ossify and does so primarily through intramembranous ossification.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The carpal bone is not present in the proximal row is:
explanation: Capitate: The proximal row consists of the Scaphoid, Lunate, Triquetral, and Pisiform. The Capitate is the largest carpal bone and is located in the distal row, articulating with the 3rd metacarpal.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The muscle not included in the superficial compartment of forearm:
explanation: Flexor pollicis longus: The superficial flexor group includes the Pronator Teres, FCR, Palmaris Longus, FDS, and FCU. The Flexor Pollicis Longus (FPL) is located in the deep compartment alongside the Flexor Digitorum Profundus.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Nerve supply to trapezius is from:
explanation: Spinal accessory nerve: The trapezius receives its motor innervation from the Spinal Accessory Nerve (CN XI). It is anatomically distinct from the upper limb muscles supplied by the brachial plexus.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The tendons of the following muscle form the rotator cuff except:
explanation: Teres major: The rotator cuff "SITS" muscles (Supraspinatus, Infraspinatus, Teres minor, Subscapularis) stabilize the joint. Teres major is a prime mover that acts on the humerus but does not reinforce the joint capsule.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The radial nerve:

options.a: Supplies sensation to medial side of dorsum of hand.
options.b: Arises from upper and middle trunks of brachial plexus
options.c: May be damaged by fractures of neck of humerus.

explanation: Supplies extensor muscles: The radial nerve is the nerve of the posterior compartment. It provides motor supply to the triceps brachii in the arm and all the extensor muscles of the forearm.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The nerve most likely to be injured in fracture of the midshaft of humerus is:
explanation: Radial nerve: The radial nerve travels in the spiral groove directly against the midshaft of the humerus. This anatomical proximity makes it the most vulnerable structure to injury during humeral shaft fractures, leading to loss of extensor function.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Direct attachment of pectoral girdle to trunk is by the following muscles except:
explanation: Pectoralis major: Muscles like Pectoralis minor and Trapezius attach the pectoral girdle (scapula/clavicle) to the trunk. The Pectoralis major is excluded because it attaches the humerus (arm) to the trunk, bypassing the direct girdle-to-trunk stabilization.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The back of the medial epicondyle of humerus is related to the:
explanation: Ulnar nerve: The ulnar nerve passes posterior to the medial epicondyle in the ulnar groove (the "funny bone"). It is easily palpated here and susceptible to compression or "tardy ulnar palsy" following elbow fractures.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The thoracoacromial artery is a branch of:
explanation: Second part of axillary artery: The axillary artery is divided by the Pectoralis minor; the second part (deep to the muscle) gives off the thoracoacromial artery and the lateral thoracic artery.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Muscle responsible for stabilization of the shoulder joint:
explanation: Long Head of triceps brachii: While the SITS muscles are the main stabilizers, the long head of triceps provides significant inferior stability to the shoulder joint when the arm is abducted. It supports the humeral head from below at the infraglenoid tubercle.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Supination and flexion at elbow joint is caused by:
explanation: Biceps brachii: The Biceps brachii is a powerful flexor of the elbow and the strongest supinator of the forearm when the elbow is flexed.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Damage to this nerve results in wrist drop:
explanation: Radial nerve: The radial nerve supplies all the extensors of the wrist and fingers. Damage (often at the spiral groove) results in wrist drop, where the patient cannot extend the wrist against gravity.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The nerve injured due to fracture of surgical neck of humerus:
explanation: Axillary nerve: The axillary nerve (C5, C6) and the posterior circumflex humeral artery wrap around the surgical neck of the humerus. Fractures here risk nerve damage, leading to deltoid paralysis and sensory loss over the "regimental badge" area.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The common direction of dislocations of the shoulders joint is:
explanation: Inferior: The shoulder joint is weakest at its inferior aspect because it lacks support from the rotator cuff (SITS muscles). Consequently, most dislocations occur in an anteroinferior direction, often stretching the axillary nerve.
subject: Anatomy

statement: In fractures of the medial epicondyle of the humerus, the nerve most likely to be injured is:
explanation: Ulnar: The ulnar nerve runs in a shallow groove on the posterior aspect of the medial epicondyle. Fractures or severe trauma to this bony prominence directly impact the nerve, potentially leading to ulnar-claw deformity.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Wasting of thenar eminence may be caused by damage of:
explanation: Median nerve: The median nerve supplies the thenar muscles (O-A-F: Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, and Flexor pollicis brevis). Chronic compression or injury leads to thenar atrophy, giving the hand a flattened "Ape Hand" appearance.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The nerve involved in carpal tunnel syndrome is:
explanation: Median nerve: Carpal tunnel syndrome is caused by the compression of the median nerve as it passes beneath the flexor retinaculum. This leads to pain, numbness in the lateral three and a half fingers, and thenar weakness.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The femoral sheath is a continuation of the:
explanation: Transversalis fascia: The femoral sheath is a funnel-shaped fascial tube formed by the downward extension of the abdominal linings: the transversalis fascia (anteriorly) and the fascia iliaca (posteriorly).
subject: Anatomy

statement: Axillary nerve supplies to:
explanation: Teres minor: The axillary nerve (C5, C6) provides motor innervation to two muscles: the deltoid and the teres minor. It also provides sensory supply to the skin over the lower deltoid.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Lateral cord of brachial plexus supplies to:
explanation: Pectoralis major: The lateral pectoral nerve, a branch of the lateral cord, supplies the pectoralis major. While the medial cord also supplies it via the medial pectoral nerve, the lateral cord is a primary motor source for this muscle.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Damage to which nerve causes loss of flexion of forearm at elbow joint:
explanation: Musculocutaneous nerve: This nerve supplies the "BBC" muscles: Biceps brachii, Brachialis, and Coracobrachialis. Since the biceps and brachialis are the primary flexors of the elbow, damage to this nerve severely inhibits flexion.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The acromioclavicular joint is strengthened by the:
explanation: Coracoclavicular ligament: Although the acromioclavicular ligament is part of the joint capsule, the coracoclavicular ligament (conoid and trapezoid parts) is the primary structure providing strength and transmitting weight between the scapula and clavicle.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Clavipectoral fascia is pierced by all EXCEPT:

options.e: Thoraco acromial branch of axillary artery

explanation: Medial pectoral nerve: The clavipectoral fascia is pierced by the Lateral pectoral nerve, Acromiothoracic (thoracoacromial) artery, Cephalic vein, and Lymphatics (LACL). The medial pectoral nerve pierces the pectoralis minor and major further down.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which two structures pass through quadrangular space:

options.a: Musculocutaneous nerve and posterior circumflex humeral vessels
options.c: Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery
options.d: Radial nerve anterior circumflex humeral artery

explanation: Axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery: The quadrangular space serves as a neurovascular gateway from the axilla to the posterior shoulder. The axillary nerve and posterior circumflex humeral artery pass through it together to reach and supply the deltoid and teres minor.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which muscle is innervated by both the ulnar and median nerves:
explanation: Flexor digitorum profundus: This is a "hybrid" muscle. The lateral half (index and middle fingers) is supplied by the median nerve, while the medial half (ring and little fingers) is supplied by the ulnar nerve. This dual supply is why ulnar nerve injury only affects the medial side of the "claw".
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which muscles form the posterior wall of axilla:
explanation: Subscapularis, latissimus dorsi & teres major: The posterior wall of the axilla is formed primarily by the subscapularis (upper part), and the latissimus dorsi and teres major (lower part). These muscles form the posterior axillary fold.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The most common site of fracture of the clavicle is:

options.d: Junction of the medial two thirds and the lateral third
options.e: Junction of the lateral two thirds and the medial third

explanation: Junction of the medial two-thirds and the lateral third: This is the weakest point of the clavicle because it is where the bone changes its curvature from convex anteriorly to concave posteriorly. It also lacks the protection of the subclavius muscle at this specific point.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which structure Is not the content of cubital fossa:
explanation: Cephalic vein: The cubital fossa contents (medial to lateral) are the Median nerve, Brachial artery, Biceps tendon, and Radial nerve. The cephalic vein is a superficial vein that lies in the lateral bicipital groove, outside the deep boundaries of the fossa.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following structures cross superficial to the flexor retinaculum at the wrist:
explanation: Palmaris longus: The median nerve and deep flexor tendons pass deep to the flexor retinaculum through the carpal tunnel. The Palmaris longus tendon and the ulnar nerve/artery pass superficial to it.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The long thoracic nerve innervates which of the following muscles:
explanation: Serratus anterior: The long thoracic nerve (C5, C6, C7) descends on the superficial surface of the serratus anterior. Damage to this nerve causes "winging of the scapula" because the muscle can no longer protract the scapula against the ribs.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following tendons are inserted into the base of the proximal phalanx of the thumb EXCEPT:
explanation: Opponens pollicis: The Opponens pollicis inserts along the whole length of the lateral border of the first metacarpal bone, not the phalanx. Its specific job is to rotate the metacarpal to face the fingers (opposition).
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following bones form the  proximal row of carpal bones EXCEPT:
explanation: Trapezium: The proximal row includes the Scaphoid, Lunate, Triquetrum, and Pisiform. The Trapezium is part of the distal row and is easily identified because it articulates with the thumb metacarpal.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The tendons of the following muscle form the rotator cuff EXCEPT:
explanation: Teres major: While the Teres minor is one of the four "SITS" muscles that stabilize the shoulder joint capsule (rotator cuff), the Teres major is a larger extrinsic muscle that acts as a prime mover (adduction/medial rotation) without attaching to the capsule.
subject: Anatomy

statement: All of the following bones develop intra cartilaginous ossification EXCEPT:
explanation: Clavicle: While most long bones of the limb (humerus, radius, ulna) develop via endochondral (intracartilaginous) ossification, the clavicle is unique. It is the first bone to ossify and does so primarily through intramembranous ossification, though its ends do have secondary cartilaginous centers.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The radial nerve:

options.a: Supplies sensation to medial side of dorsum of hand
options.b: Arises from upper and middle trunks of brachial plexus
options.c: May be damaged by fractures of neck of humerus

explanation: Supplies extensor muscles: The radial nerve is the nerve of the posterior compartment. It supplies the triceps in the arm and all the muscles in the posterior compartment of the forearm, which are responsible for extension of the wrist and fingers.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following statement are true about the mammary gland EXCEPT:

options.c: Lymphatic drainage is to the axillary lymph nodes
options.e: Ligaments of cooper anchor it to pectoral fascia

explanation: Modified sebaceous gland: The mammary gland is anatomically a modified sweat gland (apocrine type). Sebaceous glands are oil-producing glands associated with hair follicles, whereas the breast is designed for milk secretion.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The pectoralis major muscle:

options.a: Is innervated by the thoracodorsal nerve
options.b: Is an abductor of the arm
options.c: Originates from the lateral third of the clavicle
options.d: Is a powerful flexor of the forearm
options.e: Is an adductor of the arm

explanation: Adductor of the arm: The Pectoralis major is a powerful adductor and medial rotator of the humerus. It also assists in flexion (clavicular head). It cannot abduct the arm, as its vector pulls the humerus toward the midline.
subject: Anatomy

statement: All of the following structures are located on the proximal at the proximal end of the humerus except:
explanation: Olecranon process: The olecranon process is a feature of the proximal ulna, forming the bony point of the elbow. The proximal humerus consists of the head, anatomical/surgical necks, and the greater/lesser tubercles.
subject: Anatomy

statement: This nerve arises from the medial cord of the brachial plexus:
explanation: Median nerve: The median nerve has a dual origin. While it receives a lateral root from the lateral cord, its medial root arises directly from the medial cord (C8, T1). The other listed nerves arise from the posterior cord (Axillary, Radial) or lateral cord (Musculocutaneous).
subject: Anatomy

statement: The structure that holds the head of ulna against the radius is the:
explanation: Annular ligament: The annular ligament serves as a "sling" that holds the head of the radius against the radial notch of the ulna. This allows the radius to pivot during pronation and supination while staying attached to the ulna.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following statements about the glenohumeral joint is true:

options.a: The glenoid fossa is larger than the humeral head
options.b: The tendon of the subscapularis muscle reinforces the posterior side of the joint
options.c: The glenohumeral ligaments strongly support the joint
options.d: The supraspinatus tendon crosses superior to the joint capsule
options.e: The tendon of the short head of the biceps brachii muscle passes through the joint

explanation: Supraspinatus tendon crosses superior to the joint capsule: The supraspinatus tendon passes beneath the acromion and directly superior to the glenohumeral joint capsule to insert on the greater tubercle. It is the most commonly injured part of the rotator cuff due to this narrow passage.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following belongs to diarthrosis variety of joints:
explanation: Synovial joints, or diarthroses, are freely movable joints. Examples include the shoulder, hip, and knee. These joints allow movement in one or more axes and are the most mobile type of joint in the body.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding Joints all of the following statements are true except:

options.b: The joint between tooth and its bony socket is called gomphosis
options.c: Synovial joints are partially moveable joints
options.d: Schindylesis is a type of suture joint.

explanation: All statements about joints are correct except the one stating synovial joints are partially movable. Synovial joints are freely movable, whereas symphyses are secondary cartilaginous, gomphosis is fibrous, and schindylesis is a type of suture.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The following are ball and socket joints:
explanation: Ball and socket joints, such as the shoulder and hip, allow multiaxial movement in almost all directions. Other joints like the knee, ankle, or wrist are hinge or condyloid types, not ball-and-socket.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding joints all of the following are true except:
explanation: All statements about joints are true except that a suture is secondary cartilaginous. Sutures are immobile fibrous joints connecting skull bones. Gomphosis is fibrous, and synchondrosis is a primary cartilaginous joint.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The synovial joints:

options.a: have synovial membranes covering articular surface of bones
options.b: Have hyaline cartilage covering the articular surface of bone
options.c: Are capsules made u p o f skeletal muscle fibers
options.d: Contain synovial fluid secreted by fibrous capsule of joint.

explanation: The articular surfaces of synovial joints are covered by hyaline cartilage, which reduces friction and absorbs mechanical shock. The synovial fluid in the joint cavity lubricates the surfaces, but it is secreted by the synovial membrane, not the fibrous capsule.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Following are the types of synovial joint except:
explanation: All major types of synovial joints include saddle, pivot, ellipsoid, ball-and-socket, hinge, and plane joints. The joint between vertebral bodies is not a synovial joint; it is a secondary cartilaginous joint (symphysis).
subject: Anatomy

statement: A symphysis:
explanation: A symphysis is a secondary cartilaginous joint containing a fibrocartilaginous disc. This disc allows slight movement and absorbs compressive forces, such as at the pubic symphysis or intervertebral discs.
subject: Anatomy

statement: A symphysis:
explanation: Symphysis is always located in the median plane of the body. It is a slightly movable, secondary cartilaginous joint, not meant for wide-range movement, and it joins bones like the two pubic bones.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Circumduction is a movement that involves:
explanation: Circumduction is a circular movement that combines flexion, extension, abduction, and adduction. It occurs at ball-and-socket joints such as the shoulder and hip, which allow movement in multiple axes.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following joints is ellipsoid:
explanation: The wrist joint (radiocarpal joint) is an ellipsoid joint, which allows movement in two planes: flexion-extension and abduction-adduction, but no axial rotation.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Syndesmosis is a type of:
explanation: Syndesmosis is a fibrous joint where bones are connected by interosseous ligaments, allowing slight movement. Examples include the distal tibiofibular joint.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding joints all of the following statements are true except:

options.c: Joint between tooth & socket is gomphosis
options.d: In syndesmosis, interosseous ligament is found b/w bones

explanation: Synovial joints are freely movable, while symphyses are secondary cartilaginous, gomphosis is fibrous, and syndesmosis has interosseous ligaments.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which one of the following is not a fibrous joint:
explanation: Among the listed options, symphysis is not a fibrous joint; it is a secondary cartilaginous joint with fibrocartilage, while suture, syndesmosis, and gomphosis are all fibrous joints.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which characteristic is false regarding the knee joint:
explanation: The knee is a complex bicondylar hinge joint, allowing mainly flexion and extension with some rotation. It is not uniaxial, so the statement “uniaxial” is false.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Epiphyseal growth plate is:
explanation: The epiphyseal plate is a primary cartilaginous joint (synchondrosis) made of hyaline cartilage that undergoes endochondral ossification, allowing longitudinal growth of bones.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Choose incorrect match:
explanation: Plane suture does not occur between a tooth and its socket; that is a gomphosis. Other suture types like serrate, denticulate, and squamous are correctly matched with cranial sutures.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of following is not an interosseous ligament:
explanation: Cruciate ligaments of the knee are not interosseous ligaments. Interosseous ligaments connect adjacent bones (e.g., stylohyoid, coracoclavicular, ligamenta flava) while cruciate ligaments stabilize the knee joint internally.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a synchondroses:
explanation: The manubriosternal joint is a secondary cartilaginous joint (symphysis), not a synchondrosis. Synchondroses are primary cartilaginous joints formed by hyaline cartilage, like the epiphyseal plates or cranial base joints.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is found in synchondrosis:
explanation: Synchondroses are composed of hyaline cartilage, which allows slight growth or movement and acts as a temporary connection between bones during development.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is found in Symphysis:
explanation: symphysis is a secondary cartilaginous joint with fibrocartilage connecting the bones, providing shock absorption and slight mobility, as seen in the pubic symphysis or intervertebral discs.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Meniscus is a wedge-shaped fibrocartilage found in:
explanation: The meniscus is a wedge-shaped fibrocartilage found in the knee joint, improving joint congruity, absorbing shock, and stabilizing the femorotibial articulation.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a complex joint:
explanation: The elbow joint is not complex; it is a hinge joint connecting humerus with ulna and radius. Complex joints have additional intra-articular structures, unlike the elbow.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is a compound joint:
explanation: The elbow joint is a compound joint because it involves three bones: humerus, radius, and ulna, working together in a single joint capsule.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a plane joint:
explanation: Plane joints allow gliding movements between flat or slightly curved surfaces, like intercarpal or intertarsal joints. The knee joint is a hinge/bicondylar joint and not a plane joint.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Select incorrect pair:
explanation: The wrist joint is not a sellar (saddle) joint; it is an ellipsoid (condyloid) joint. Sellar joints are present at the thumb carpometacarpal joint.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a saddle joint:
explanation: The 1st carpometacarpal joint and calcaneocuboidal joint are saddle joints. The metacarpophalangeal joint is a condyloid (ellipsoid) joint, allowing flexion, extension, abduction, and adduction.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding Muscle all of the above statements are correct except:

options.c: Skeletal muscles are in voluntary control
options.d: Smooth muscles of ciliary body are voluntary

explanation: Smooth muscles, including those in the ciliary body, are involuntary. They are spindle-shaped, while cardiac muscles are striated and skeletal muscles are under voluntary control.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Regarding the muscle all are true except:

options.d: Parallel muscle fibers have great range of movement

explanation: Muscles with parallel fibers, like the sartorius, generally allow a greater range of movement. The statement “parallel fibers have great range” is false in general for all muscles because fiber arrangement varies.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Epimysium Covers:
explanation: The epimysium is a connective tissue sheath that surrounds the entire muscle, providing protection and transmitting force to tendons. Fascicles and individual fibers have separate sheaths: perimysium and endomysium.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Following is a fan shaped muscle:
explanation: The temporalis muscle is fan-shaped, with fibers spreading from a broad origin and converging to a narrow insertion. Other muscles listed are not fan shaped.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which condition is false regarding condition of muscle atrophy:
explanation: Muscle atrophy occurs due to denervation, chronic illness, or lower motor neuron disease. Weightlifting leads to hypertrophy, not atrophy.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Tendon is made up of:
explanation: Tendons are made of dense regular collagen fibers, arranged in parallel for high tensile strength, allowing them to transmit force from muscle to bone.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Select the incorrect match:

options.a: Hemiplegia (paralysis of' one half of the body

explanation: Hemiparesis is weakness of one side of the body, not both shoulders. Hemiplegia is paralysis of one half, paraplegia of lower limbs, and quadriplegia of all four limbs.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a spiral muscle:
explanation: Spiral muscles twist around the axis of the limb, e.g., latissimus dorsi, pectoralis major, supinator. Serratus anterior is not spiral; it is a fan-shaped or flat muscle.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a triangular muscle:
explanation: Triangular muscles have a broad origin converging to a narrow insertion. Frontalis is flat, not triangular, while deltoid, temporalis, and adductor longus are triangular.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is not a uni-pennate muscle:
explanation: Rectus femoris is bipennate, not unipennate. Unipennate muscles include flexor pollicis longus, extensor digitorum, and peroneus tertius.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is bipennate muscle:
explanation: Rectus femoris has fibers on both sides of a central tendon, making it bipennate, allowing stronger force production.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is a multipennate muscle:
explanation: Subscapularis is multipennate, with multiple central tendons and fibers inserting obliquely, maximizing force.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is a circumpennate muscle:
explanation: Tibialis anterior is circumpennate, meaning fibers radiate around a central tendon, allowing both strength and some flexibility.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following is a cruciate muscle:
explanation: Cruciate muscles have fibers crossing in an X-shaped pattern, like masseter and sternocleidomastoid, allowing multidirectional force.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The sarcoplasmic reticulum:

options.a: Is a part of neuromuscular junction
options.d: Releases calcium which allows actin to bind with myosin

explanation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum stores and releases calcium ions, enabling actin-myosin interaction and muscle contraction; it is continuous with T-tubules.
subject: Anatomy

statement: The neuromuscular junction is a specialized form of synaptic contact between motor neuron axon terminal and muscle. Which of the following properties distinguish it from the CNS synapse:

options.a: The presence of junctional folds of the postsynaptic membrane
options.b: The presence of synaptic vesicles within the presynaptic axon
options.c: The presence of postsynaptic receptor membrane

explanation: The neuromuscular junction has junctional folds in the postsynaptic membrane, increasing surface area for acetylcholine receptors, distinguishing it from CNS synapses.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Paraplegia is the paralysis of:
explanation: Paraplegia refers to paralysis of the lower half of the body, usually due to spinal cord injury. Hemiplegia affects one side; quadriplegia affects all four limbs.
subject: Anatomy

statement: Which of the following features is characteristic of skeletal muscle but not smooth muscle:

options.a: Presence of actin and myosin filaments
options.d: Requirement of calcium ions for contraction

explanation: Skeletal muscle has sarcomeres, the contractile units giving striations, which are absent in smooth muscle. Both have actin, myosin, and require calcium.
subject: Anatomy
