statement: Which of the following organelles is first affected in myocardial infarction:
explanation: Ribosomes are first affected in ischemia. Protein synthesis decreases early. This contributes to cell injury.
subject: Pathology

statement: A 60-year-old man presents with crushing substernal chest pain radiating to the neck and arm with elevated troponin I levels. If he dies the next day, which histologic feature would most likely be found in the myocardium:

options.c: Slight swelling of tissue and change of color
options.d: Young fibroblasts and new vessels growing into the infarcted tissue

explanation: Within 1–3 days post-MI, neutrophils infiltrate necrotic tissue. Coagulative necrosis is present. This is acute phase of MI.
subject: Pathology

statement: Organs less susceptible to infarction because of dual blood supply:
explanation: Organs with dual blood supply like lungs and liver are protected from infarction. Even if one supply is blocked, the other maintains perfusion. This reduces ischemic damage.
subject: Pathology

statement: A 64-year-old woman with long-standing chronic obstructive pulmonary disease develops cor pulmonale with right-sided heart failure. What is the most likely cause:

options.b: Disease of the lungs or pulmonary vessels

explanation: Cor pulmonale results from lung disease causing pulmonary hypertension. This increases right ventricular workload. Eventually leads to right-sided heart failure.
subject: Pathology

statement: A factor that stimulates proliferation of smooth muscle cells and contributes to the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis:
explanation: PDGF stimulates smooth muscle proliferation in vessel walls. This contributes to plaque formation in atherosclerosis. It is released from platelets and macrophages.
subject: Pathology

statement: A 60-year-old man returns to normal activities four days after myocardial infarction. At this time, there is a special danger of:
explanation: Myocardial rupture occurs 3–7 days post-MI. Due to macrophage-mediated weakening of tissue. It can lead to sudden death.
subject: Pathology

statement: Which of the following does not predispose to thrombosis:
explanation: Thrombocytopenia decreases clot formation. Other options are components of Virchow’s triad and promote thrombosis.
subject: Pathology

statement: Paradoxical emboli are:

options.c: Caused by sudden atmospheric pressure changes
options.d: Due to presence of an interventricular or interatrial defect

explanation: Paradoxical emboli pass from venous to arterial circulation. This occurs via septal defects like ASD or VSD. They bypass the lungs.
subject: Pathology

statement: The following predispose to deep vein thrombosis except:
explanation: Factor VIII deficiency causes bleeding tendency. Immobilization and surgery promote stasis and thrombosis. Hence A is not a risk.
subject: Pathology

statement: A 55-year-old woman presents with chest pain that predictably occurs during exertion and is relieved by rest. Which condition best describes her state:
explanation: Stable angina occurs due to fixed atherosclerotic narrowing. Pain appears with exertion and is relieved by rest. No myocardial necrosis occurs.
subject: Pathology

statement: An 86-year-old man with recurrent urinary tract infections presents with fever, tachycardia, hypotension, and altered mental status. Which type of shock is most likely:
explanation: Septic shock results from severe infection. Causes vasodilation and hypotension. Associated with fever and altered mental status.
subject: Pathology

statement: A bedridden elderly patient suddenly develops pleuritic chest pain and hemoptysis. The most likely site of origin of the embolus is:
explanation: Most pulmonary emboli originate from DVT in lower limbs. Emboli travel to lungs and cause infarction and hemoptysis.
subject: Pathology

statement: A 41-year-old woman presents with severe headache, visual disturbances, and blood pressure of 220/150 mmHg. These findings are most likely associated with:
explanation: Severe hypertension causes hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis. It shows onion-skin appearance due to smooth muscle proliferation.
subject: Pathology

statement: At autopsy, the pathologist identifies “heart failure cells.” This finding results from:

options.b: Chronic passive congestion of the lungs

explanation: Heart failure cells are hemosiderin-laden macrophages. Seen in chronic pulmonary congestion due to left-sided heart failure.
subject: Pathology

statement: Irreversible myocardial injury occurs after approximately:
explanation: Irreversible injury begins after 20–30 minutes of ischemia. Leads to cell death and necrosis. Early intervention is crucial.
subject: Pathology

statement: Most frequent site of venous thrombosis:
explanation: DVT most commonly occurs in deep veins of lower limbs. Due to stasis and immobilization. It can lead to pulmonary embolism.
subject: Pathology

statement: Which of the following is not a cause of thrombosis:
explanation: Antithrombin III is anticoagulant and prevents clotting. Endothelial damage and polycythemia promote thrombosis.
subject: Pathology

statement: Postmenopausal women have greater risk of:
explanation: Estrogen has protective cardiovascular effects. After menopause, risk of atherosclerosis increases. Leads to higher CV disease risk.
subject: Pathology

statement: Two days after myocardial infarction, histology of the heart shows:
explanation: At 1–3 days post-MI, neutrophils infiltrate necrotic myocardium. This is acute inflammatory phase of infarction.
subject: Pathology

statement: The most frequent cause of cor pulmonale with isolated right-sided heart failure is:

options.a: Disease of the lungs or pulmonary vessels

explanation: Cor pulmonale is due to chronic lung disease. It increases pulmonary pressure. Leads to right-sided failure.
subject: Pathology

statement: In a state of shock, there is:

options.b: Decreased hydrostatic pressure and increased osmotic pressure

explanation: Shock is characterized by circulatory collapse. There is inadequate tissue perfusion. Leads to organ dysfunction.
subject: Pathology

statement: The most common cause of death immediately following acute myocardial infarction is:
explanation: Fatal arrhythmias are most common early cause of death post-MI. Especially ventricular fibrillation.
subject: Pathology

statement: Onion-skin appearance of blood vessels is seen in:
explanation: Hyperplastic arteriolosclerosis shows concentric smooth muscle proliferation. Gives onion-skin appearance. Seen in severe hypertension.
subject: Pathology

statement: First visible gross evidence for formation of atheroma:
explanation: Fatty streaks are earliest lesions of atherosclerosis. Composed of lipid-laden foam cells. Seen even in young individuals.
subject: Pathology

statement: Hyaline arteriosclerosis is characterized by:
explanation: Hyaline arteriosclerosis shows pink hyaline thickening of vessel wall. Seen in hypertension and diabetes. Causes luminal narrowing.
subject: Pathology

statement: Most common symptom of myocardial infarction:
explanation: Chest pain is the most common symptom of MI. It is crushing, substernal, and may radiate. Due to myocardial ischemia.
subject: Pathology

statement: Type of emphysema commonly associated with cigarette smoking:
explanation: Centriacinar emphysema affects the respiratory bronchioles and is strongly linked to smoking. It predominantly involves the upper lobes. Smoking increases neutrophils and macrophages → elastase release → destruction of alveolar walls.
subject: Pathology

statement: When IgE on a mast cell Fcε receptor is cross linked by an allergen, the mast cell responds by:
explanation: Cross-linking of IgE on mast cells triggers immediate degranulation. Histamine is released causing bronchoconstriction, vasodilation, and increased vascular permeability. This is a classic type I hypersensitivity reaction.
subject: Pathology

statement: Which of the following does not cause airway narrowing in an asthma attack:
explanation: Asthma causes reversible airway obstruction due to bronchospasm, mucus plugging, and edema. Structural destruction is not typical. Airway damage occurs in emphysema, not asthma.
subject: Pathology

statement: Honeycomb appearance of lung is a characteristic finding of:
explanation: Honeycomb lung represents end-stage fibrosis with cystic airspaces. It is characteristic of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Leads to restrictive lung disease with decreased compliance.
subject: Pathology

statement: What happens to FEV1/FVC in obstructive lung diseases:
explanation: In obstructive diseases (e.g., asthma, emphysema), airway narrowing reduces FEV1 more than FVC. • This causes a decreased FEV1/FVC ratio • Reflects difficulty in expelling air • Key diagnostic feature in COPD
subject: Pathology

statement: Panacinar emphysema commonly involves which part of lung:
explanation: Panacinar emphysema is classically seen in alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency. • Affects entire acinus uniformly • Predominantly involves lower lobes • Differs from smoking-related centriacinar type
subject: Pathology

statement: Initial mediator of asthma:
explanation: Early phase of asthma is mediated by mast cell degranulation. • Histamine is released immediately • Causes bronchoconstriction and vasodilation • Later phase involves leukotrienes and eosinophils
subject: Pathology

statement: Reid index used as a criterion of quantitation in chronic bronchitis is the ratio of thickness of:
explanation: Reid index measures gland enlargement in chronic bronchitis. • Normal <40% • Increased in chronic bronchitis due to mucus hypersecretion • Reflects glandular hypertrophy
subject: Pathology

statement: What is the normal value of Reid index:
explanation: Reid index is normally less than 40%. • Measures proportion of mucous glands • Increased (>50%) in chronic bronchitis • Indicates excessive mucus production
subject: Pathology

statement: Curshmann spirals are seen in sputum in:
explanation: Curschmann spirals are mucus plugs from bronchioles. • Seen in asthma sputum • Associated with airway obstruction • Often accompanied by eosinophils
subject: Pathology

statement: Which one of the following is associated with increased lung compliance:
explanation: Emphysema destroys elastic tissue of lungs. • Leads to increased compliance (easy inflation) • But poor recoil → air trapping • Opposite in fibrosis (stiff lungs)
subject: Pathology

statement: A 55-year-old heavy smoker presents with severe dyspnea, dry cough, barrel chest, prolonged expiration, and decreased FEV1. What is the principal pathogenetic mechanism:

options.a: Production of autoantibodies against alveolar basement membrane
options.c: Protease production by neutrophils and macrophages

explanation: Smoking causes inflammation → protease release. • Elastase destroys alveolar walls • Imbalance of protease-antiprotease system • Leads to emphysema and airspace enlargement
subject: Pathology

statement: Bronchogenic carcinoma has increased incidence in pneumoconiosis. Most likely association is with:
explanation: Asbestos exposure increases cancer risk. • Strongly associated with bronchogenic carcinoma • Also causes mesothelioma • Risk increases with smoking
subject: Pathology

statement: In the pathogenesis of bronchiectasis, susceptibility to infection is due to:

options.b: Accumulation of thick and viscid secretions obstructing airways

explanation: Obstruction leads to infection in bronchiectasis. • Thick mucus traps bacteria • Causes chronic infection and inflammation • Leads to permanent bronchial dilation
subject: Pathology

statement: Hunched-over posture with “pink puffer” appearance is characteristic of:
explanation: “Pink puffers” are emphysema patients. • Maintain oxygenation by hyperventilation • Thin, dyspneic, barrel chest • Contrast: “blue bloater” in chronic bronchitis
subject: Pathology

statement: All of the following are obstructive lung diseases except:
explanation: Pulmonary fibrosis is a restrictive disease. • Causes stiff lungs and ↓ compliance • Obstructive diseases cause airflow limitation • Key distinction in PFTs
subject: Pathology

statement: A 4-year-old child has seasonal breathing difficulty with eosinophilia and Charcot-Leyden crystals in sputum. Which mediator is responsible for bronchospasm:
explanation: Leukotrienes are potent bronchoconstrictors. • Released in late-phase asthma • Cause sustained bronchospasm • Also increase mucus and edema
subject: Pathology

statement: A 28-year-old man presents with infertility, bronchiectasis, and dextrocardia. What is the diagnosis:
explanation: Kartagener = primary ciliary dyskinesia. • Triad: bronchiectasis, sinusitis, situs inversus • Defective ciliary movement • Leads to infertility and infections
subject: Pathology

statement: A 22-year-old female develops sudden dyspnea with wheezing. Sputum shows Curschmann spirals and Charcot-Leyden crystals with eosinophils. What is the diagnosis:
explanation: Classic findings of asthma. • Eosinophils + Charcot-Leyden crystals • Curschmann spirals (mucus plugs) • Reversible airway obstruction
subject: Pathology

statement: A 50-year-old man with progressive dyspnea shows restrictive pattern on pulmonary function tests and interstitial linear opacities on X-ray. Which pathology is most likely:

options.a: Chronic inflammation with eosinophils in bronchi
options.b: Hyperplasia of mucous glands in bronchi
options.c: Uniform dilation of air spaces distal to bronchioles

explanation: Restrictive disease → fibrosis. • Interstitial fibrosis causes stiff lungs • Honeycomb pattern on imaging • Seen in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
subject: Pathology

statement: A 60-year-old man presents with chronic productive cough and copious purulent sputum. CT shows bronchial dilatation and wall thickening. What is the diagnosis:
explanation: Bronchiectasis = permanent bronchial dilation. • Chronic infection + obstruction • Copious foul sputum • Seen on CT as dilated bronchi
subject: Pathology

statement: A 61-year-old man presents with shortness of breath and chest X-ray shows diffuse pulmonary infiltrates with patchy interstitial fibrosis. What is the diagnosis:
explanation: IPF causes progressive fibrosis. • Restrictive lung disease • Interstitial infiltrates on imaging • Leads to honeycomb lung
subject: Pathology

statement: A newborn of a diabetic mother presents with respiratory distress and ground glass opacity on chest X-ray. What is the diagnosis:
explanation: Due to surfactant deficiency. • Common in infants of diabetic mothers • Causes alveolar collapse • “Ground glass” appearance on X-ray
subject: Pathology

statement: What is the common cause of panacinar emphysema:
explanation: A1AT deficiency → loss of antiprotease activity. • Leads to unchecked elastase activity • Causes panacinar emphysema • Predominantly affects lower lobes
subject: Pathology

statement: Which route of administration is commonly used for insulin:
explanation: Insulin is a protein that would be degraded by digestive enzymes if taken orally, and it requires a route that allows for consistent absorption. The subcutaneous route is preferred because it provides a slow and steady release into the bloodstream, which is ideal for managing blood glucose levels throughout the day. Intravenous administration is typically reserved for emergency settings like diabetic ketoacidosis, while intramuscular injections are generally avoided due to faster and less predictable absorption rates.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: A molecule that interacts as an activator or inhibitor and brings about a change in biological function through its chemical actions is known as:
explanation: This definition encompasses both activators (agonists) and inhibitors (antagonists) that bring about their effects through chemical actions. While hormones and neurotransmitters are signaling molecules, "drug" is the most comprehensive term for any exogenous or endogenous molecule used as a therapeutic or experimental agent.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which type of transmembrane signaling receptors are used by Vitamin D:
explanation: Vitamin D is a lipid-soluble molecule that can easily cross the plasma membrane to interact with intracellular receptors located in the cytoplasm or nucleus. Once bound, the receptor-hormone complex acts as a transcription factor, binding to specific DNA sequences to regulate gene expression. This mechanism is distinct from G-protein coupled or membrane-spanning receptors, which are used by water-soluble signaling molecules that cannot enter the cell.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: All of the following factors directly affect drug absorption except:
explanation: Drug absorption is significantly influenced by physiological variables like pH (which affects ionization), blood flow to the site, and the contact time at the absorption surface. While molecular weight does influence the rate of passive diffusion, it is often considered an intrinsic physical property of the drug rather than a "direct" environmental factor at the absorption site.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following is not a mechanism of absorption of drugs from the GI tract:
explanation: Most drugs are absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract via passive diffusion, facilitated diffusion, or active transport systems. Pinocytosis, the process of a cell "drinking" or engulfing fluid, is an extremely rare and inefficient mechanism for drug absorption, generally reserved for very large molecules or specialized proteins. Because the vast majority of therapeutic agents rely on smaller, more efficient transport pathways, pinocytosis is not considered a standard mechanism for GI drug uptake.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: For a drug given orally, the principal site of drug absorption is:
explanation: The small intestine is the primary site for the absorption of orally administered drugs because it offers a massive surface area due to the presence of villi and microvilli. Additionally, the high blood flow and relatively neutral pH environment compared to the stomach favor the absorption of a wide range of chemical compounds. While the stomach can absorb some drugs (like aspirin or alcohol), the sheer surface area of the small intestine makes it the most significant contributor to bioavailability.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: A drug that binds to the inactive state of a receptor & decreases constitutive activity is known as:
explanation: An inverse agonist is a drug that binds to the same receptor as an agonist but produces a pharmacological response opposite to that of the agonist. It specifically targets receptors that exhibit constitutive activity, meaning they are active even in the absence of a ligand, and forces them into an inactive state. This differs from a standard antagonist, which merely blocks the effects of an agonist without affecting the receptor's baseline activity.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Type of agonist which acts as antagonist in presence of full agonist is known as:
explanation: A partial agonist is a molecule that binds to a receptor but produces only a sub-maximal response, even at full receptor occupancy. In the presence of a full agonist, a partial agonist can actually act as an antagonist by competing for the same binding sites and reducing the overall effect.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: IV administration of drugs:
explanation: Drugs administered via the intravenous (IV) route bypass the barriers of absorption and the liver's first-pass metabolism entirely, resulting in 100% bioavailability. This ensures that the entire dose enters the systemic circulation immediately.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Type of agonist which decreases or abolishes constitutive activity of receptor:
explanation: An inverse agonist is a drug that binds to the same receptor as an agonist but produces a pharmacological response opposite to that of the agonist. It specifically targets receptors that exhibit constitutive activity, meaning they are active even in the absence of a ligand, and forces them into an inactive state. This differs from a standard antagonist, which merely blocks the effects of an agonist without affecting the receptor's baseline activity.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: A study was carried out in isolated skeletal muscle preparations to determine the action of a new drug "succinylcholine," which in separate studies is bound to the same receptors as acetylcholine. In the absence of succinylcholine, acetylcholine caused contraction of the muscle & in presence of succinylcholine, acetylcholine did not cause contraction. But when the concentration of acetylcholine was increased, muscle contraction occurred. Which of the following best describes succinylcholine:
explanation: In the scenario described the ability of increased acetylcholine concentrations to overcome the inhibitory effect of succinylcholine confirms that the two drugs are competing for the same binding site. This reversible blockade is the hallmark of a competitive antagonist, where the maximal response can still be achieved if enough agonist is present to displace the inhibitor. Although succinylcholine is clinically a depolarizing agent, within the logic of this specific experimental question, it demonstrates competitive behavior.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which statement is incorrect about drug metabolism:

options.a: All drugs are modified before their removal from the body
options.b: Action of drugs is terminated before their elimination
options.c: It is a mechanism of activation & termination of drug action
options.d: Conversion of drug to inactive form

explanation: The statement that all drugs are modified before their removal from the body is incorrect because some drugs (such as lithium or certain polar antibiotics) are excreted by the kidneys completely unchanged. While metabolism is a primary mechanism for terminating drug action or activating pro-drugs, it is not a universal requirement for elimination.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which route of administration is commonly used for vaccines:
explanation: Most vaccines are administered via the intramuscular route because muscle tissue has a rich blood supply that facilitates the transport of the vaccine to immune cells. The muscle also provides a small "depot" for the antigen, allowing for a more sustained immune response compared to direct intravenous injection.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: The action of the drug on the body is known as:
explanation: Pharmacodynamics refers to the study of the biochemical and physiological effects of drugs on the body, or simply "what the drug does to the body". This includes the study of receptor binding, signal transduction, and the ultimate functional change produced by the drug. It is the complement to pharmacokinetics, which deals with how the body processes the drug through absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Sugammadex is a new drug that reverses the action of rocuronium and certain other non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents. It interacts directly with the rocuronium molecule and not at the receptor. Which term best describes sugammadex:
explanation: Sugammadex is classified as a chemical antagonist because it works by directly encapsulating and neutralizing the rocuronium molecule in the plasma, rather than interacting with the neuromuscular receptor itself. By forming a stable complex with the drug, it rapidly lowers the concentration of free rocuronium, allowing for the reversal of neuromuscular blockade. This mechanism is distinct from pharmacologic antagonists, which must compete for receptor sites to exert their effects.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following statements about receptors is not true:

options.a: It determines the quantitative relations between dose or concentration of drug and pharmacologic effects
options.b: Responsible for the selectivity of drug action
options.c: Mediates the actions of pharmacological agonists and antagonists

explanation: Receptors are fundamental to pharmacology as they determine the quantitative relationship between drug concentration and effect, while also ensuring the selectivity of drug action. They serve as the essential mediators for both agonists and antagonists to produce a biological change.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Routes of drug excretion from the body include:
explanation: The body utilizes several organs for the removal of drugs and their metabolites, with the kidneys (urine) and liver (bile)being the primary routes. However, drugs can also be excreted through the lungs (especially volatile gases and alcohols) and the intestines. Because multiple systems contribute to the final elimination of substances from the systemic circulation, "All of these" is the correct answer.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: What is meant by constitutive activity of a receptor:

options.b: Activity in presence of partial agonist
options.c: Activity in presence of inverse agonist

explanation: An inverse agonist is a drug that binds to the same receptor as an agonist but produces a pharmacological response opposite to that of the agonist. It specifically targets receptors that exhibit constitutive activity, meaning they are active even in the absence of a ligand, and forces them into an inactive state.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Partial agonist produces less than full effect because:

options.a: It binds to activated & non-activated states of receptor
options.b: It has low affinity for receptor
options.d: It binds only to activated state of receptor

explanation: A partial agonist is a molecule that binds to a receptor but produces only a sub-maximal response, even at full receptor occupancy. In the presence of a full agonist, a partial agonist can actually act as an antagonist by competing for the same binding sites and reducing the overall effect. This limited efficacy occurs because partial agonists bind to both the activated and non-activated states of the receptor, failing to stabilize the active configuration as effectively as a full agonist.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: An 18-year-old female patient is brought to the emergency department due to drug overdose. Which route of administration is most desirable for administering an antidote:
explanation: In an emergency drug overdose situation, the intravenous route is the most desirable because it provides immediate access to the systemic circulation. This allows the antidote to reach its target receptors as quickly as possible without the delays associated with absorption from the gut or muscle.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which route of administration is used for drugs that require steady, continuous release, such as nitroglycerin:
explanation: Drugs that require a steady, continuous release over a long period, such as nitroglycerin for angina prevention, are often administered via topical transdermal patches. These patches contain a reservoir of the drug that diffuses through the skin into the bloodstream at a controlled rate, maintaining a constant therapeutic level.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which route of administration is commonly used for drugs having bitter taste:
explanation: The rectal route is a useful alternative for administering drugs that have an extremely bitter taste or cause nausea when taken orally. By bypassing the taste buds and the upper GI tract, this route avoids the unpleasant sensations and potential vomiting associated with some medications.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: A 23-year-old pregnant lady presented with Hb 10 g/dL (normal 12–16 g/dL), macrocytic anemia, an increased serum concentration of transferrin, and a normal serum concentration of vitamin B12. The most likely cause of her anemia is deficiency of which of the following:
explanation: During pregnancy, there is a high fetal demand for folate to support rapid DNA synthesis. In contrast, if the anemia is normocytic or microcytic, it is typically treated with ferrous sulfate tablets to address the increased iron requirements of both the mother and the developing fetus. Iron deficiency would result in microcytic cells rather than macrocytic ones.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Toxicity associated with acute iron poisoning usually includes which of the following:

options.d: Necrotizing gastroenteritis, shock, and metabolic acidosis

explanation: Acute iron poisoning is a medical emergency characterized by necrotizing gastroenteritis, shock, and metabolic acidosis resulting from the direct corrosive action of iron on the gut and its interference with cellular respiration.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: In a patient who requires filgrastim (G-CSF) after being treated with anticancer drugs, the therapeutic objective is to prevent which of the following:
explanation: Filgrastim stimulates the bone marrow to produce and release neutrophils. Its primary therapeutic objective following chemotherapy is to shorten the period of neutropenia and prevent life-threatening systemic infections. Additionally, it is used in clinical settings to mobilize peripheral blood stem cells, so they can be harvested for transplantation.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: The child ingested iron-containing supplements in large amount & presented with acute iron poisoning. What immediate treatment is necessary? Correction of acid-base and electrolyte abnormalities and:
explanation: This agent is a specific iron-chelating drug that binds to ferric iron in the blood to form ferrioxamine, which is then safely excreted by the kidneys. Activated charcoal is ineffective because it does not bind well to metals like iron, and other agents like dantrolene are used for entirely different conditions like malignant hyperthermia.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Treatment of Hemochromatosis due to frequent blood transfusion is:
explanation: Patients requiring frequent blood transfusions are at high risk for iron overload (hemochromatosis) because the body has no physiological mechanism to excrete the excess iron provided by the donor cells. Parenteral deferoxamine is the established treatment because it effectively chelates the systemic iron deposits and promotes their removal through the urine. Using iron supplements like iron dextran or ferumoxytol would be dangerous in this scenario as they would further exacerbate the toxic iron accumulation.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following is most likely to be required by a 5-year-old boy with chronic renal insufficiency:
explanation: A child with chronic renal failure likely suffers from anemia because the kidneys are the primary site for the production of erythropoietin. Erythropoietin is the hormone responsible for stimulating the bone marrow to produce red blood cells; thus, its deficiency leads to a significant reduction in RBC production.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs is most likely to be used to mobilize the peripheral blood stem cells needed for the patient's autologous stem cell transplantation:
explanation: Before an autologous stem cell transplant, clinicians must move (mobilize) hematopoietic stem cells from the bone marrow into the peripheral blood so they can be harvested. Filgrastim (G-CSF) is the primary drug used for this purpose because it stimulates the proliferation and migration of these cells into the systemic circulation.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Optimal treatment of normocytic or mild microcytic anemia associated with pregnancy uses which of the following:
explanation: While macrocytic anemia in pregnancy is usually due to folate deficiency, a normocytic or mild microcytic presentation is typically indicative of iron deficiency. The optimal treatment in this case is the administration of ferrous sulfate tablets to restore maternal and fetal iron stores. Erythropoietin is rarely necessary for standard pregnancy-related anemia.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Contraindications of iron therapy is:
explanation: Iron therapy is strictly contraindicated in hemolytic anemia. This is because the premature destruction of red blood cells releases their internal iron back into the systemic circulation, meaning the body is already saturated with recycled iron. Providing supplemental iron in this state can lead to rapid iron toxicity and organ damage.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following is a granulocyte colony stimulating factor:
explanation: Filgrastim is the clinical name for recombinant human granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF). It works specifically to increase the production of neutrophils, a type of white blood cell, in the bone marrow. Other options like sargramostim are GM-CSF (affecting both granulocytes and macrophages), and oprelvekin is an interleukin that specifically targets platelet production rather than granulocytes.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: A 45-year-old male stomach cancer patient underwent tumor removal surgery. After surgery, he developed megaloblastic anemia. His anemia is caused by a deficiency of X and can be treated with Y:

options.a: X = intrinsic factor; Y = folic acid
options.b: X = intrinsic factor; Y = vitamin B12
options.c: X = extrinsic factor; Y = parenteral iron

explanation: Patients who undergo surgical removal of the stomach (gastrectomy) lose the parietal cells that secrete intrinsic factor. Since intrinsic factor is essential for the absorption of vitamin B12 in the small intestine, its absence leads to a specific type of megaloblastic anemia. This condition is treated by bypassing the GI tract with parenteral Vitamin B12.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Local anesthetics cause vasodilation. Their rate and duration of action can be increased & toxicity can be decreased by:

options.d: Decreasing dose of that vasodilating anesthetic

explanation: Local anesthetics generally cause vasodilation, which increases their rate of absorption into the bloodstream, thereby shortening their duration and increasing systemic toxicity. Administering a vasoconstrictor like epinephrine is the correct strategy because it restricts local blood flow, keeping the anesthetic at the injection site longer while reducing the risk of systemic side effects.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Characteristic properties of local anesthetics include all of the following EXCEPT:

options.a: An increase in membrane refractory period

explanation: Local anesthetics function by blocking voltage-dependent sodium (Na+) channels on the inner surface of nerve membranes, which prevents the influx of ions necessary for action potential conduction. They exhibit state-dependent blockade, meaning they bind preferentially to channels in their open (activated) or inactivated states, rather than resting channels. Blocking the sodium/potassium pump or calcium channels would not produce the rapid, reversible loss of sensation characteristic of these drugs.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Local anesthetic act by:
explanation: Local anesthetics function by blocking voltage-dependent sodium (Na+) channels on the inner surface of nerve membranes, which prevents the influx of ions necessary for action potential conduction. They exhibit state-dependent blockade, meaning they bind preferentially to channels in their open (activated) or inactivated states, rather than resting channels. Blocking the sodium/potassium pump or calcium channels would not produce the rapid, reversible loss of sensation characteristic of these drugs.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following local anesthetic drugs has vasoconstrictive properties:
explanation: Cocaine is unique among local anesthetics because it possesses intrinsic vasoconstrictive properties due to its ability to inhibit the reuptake of norepinephrine; in contrast, agents like lidocaine and bupivacaine typically cause vasodilation.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: In general, local anesthetics block which fibers more rapidly:
explanation: Smaller and myelinated fibers (such as Type B and delta-Type A fibers) are blocked more rapidly than larger or unmyelinated fibers. This is because the small diameter and the presence of nodes of Ranvier in myelinated nerves allow the anesthetic to reach critical concentrations more quickly.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Local anesthetics are of two types: esters & amides. If a person is allergic to one ester local anesthetic, what should be done for local anesthesia:

options.a: Try another ester type local anesthetic
options.c: Enhance dose of ester type local anesthetic
options.d: Reduce dose of ester type local anesthetic

explanation: Local anesthetics are divided into esters and amides; if a patient is allergic to an ester-type agent the safest alternative is to give an amide-type local anesthetic.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: All of the following factors influence the action of local anesthetics EXCEPT:

options.a: Acetylcholinesterase activity in the region of the injection site
options.c: Blood flow through the tissue in which the injection is made

explanation: Factors like dose and blood flow influence their action, but acetylcholinesterase activity does not, because esters are metabolized by plasma pseudocholinesterase and amides are metabolized in the liver.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Neuromuscular blockers work by:

options.b: Binding to nicotinic receptors at NMJ
options.c: By binding to cholinergic receptors at NMJ
options.d: Enhancing access of receptor of NMJ to epinephrine

explanation: Non-depolarizing blockers, such as tubocurarine and curare, work by competitively binding to nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ). By acting as antagonists at the postsynaptic motor end plate, they prevent acetylcholine from binding and initiating muscle contraction.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs is a non-depolarizing blocker of NMJ:
explanation: Tubocurarine is the only non-depolarizing blocker in the list.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Neuromuscular paralysis that results from pharmacologic antagonism at the acetylcholine receptor of the end plate (e.g., by tubocurarine) is called:
explanation: Non-depolarizing blockers, such as tubocurarine and curare, work by competitively binding to nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ). By acting as antagonists at the postsynaptic motor end plate, they prevent acetylcholine from binding and initiating muscle contraction, a process known as a non-depolarizing or stabilizing blockade.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Succinylcholine causes depolarization by:

options.a: Activating sodium channel on postsynaptic terminal
options.b: Acting like ACh to stimulate nicotinic receptor
options.c: Activating calcium channel on presynaptic terminal

explanation: Succinylcholine is the primary example of a depolarizing blocker that acts like acetylcholine to stimulate nicotinic receptors at the motor end plate. Because it is not broken down by acetylcholinesterase, it causes persistent depolarization of the muscle fiber, leading to transient fasciculations followed by paralysis as sodium channels become inactivated.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which drug acts like a nicotinic agonist and depolarizes the neuromuscular end plate:
explanation: Succinylcholine is the primary example of a depolarizing blocker that acts like acetylcholine to stimulate nicotinic receptors at the motor end plate. Because it is not broken down by acetylcholinesterase, it causes persistent depolarization of the muscle fiber.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: In patients with myasthenia, an acute worsening of symptoms is usually relieved by:
explanation: Myasthenia gravis is characterized by an autoimmune-mediated reduction in nicotinic receptors at the NMJ, which is managed by increasing the concentration of acetylcholine via cholinesterase inhibitors like neostigmine (in order to maximize the amount of ACh present in the junction, thereby increasing the likelihood of it binding to post-synaptic receptors).
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Neostigmine differs from pilocarpine (muscarinic agonist) in having effects on:
explanation: Skeletal muscle is the correct answer as pilocarpine is unable to bind to the nicotinic receptors found there, whilst neostigmine can.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Systemic side effect of local anesthetic (Lidocaine) is:
explanation: A major systemic side effect of these drugs, particularly lidocaine, is cardiac arrhythmia, as they can block cardiac sodium channels at high concentrations. Hyperglycemia and pneumothorax are not direct pharmacological side effects of the anesthetic agents themselves.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which drug test is used to diagnose Myasthenia gravis:
explanation: To distinguish between a myasthenic crisis (under-medicated) and a cholinergic crisis (over-medicated), clinicians use the Tensilon test (edrophonium); a brief improvement in symptoms confirms the need for more medication.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs is the most effective in emergency management of malignant hyperthermia:
explanation: Dantrolene is the essential emergency treatment for malignant hyperthermia, a life-threatening reaction to certain anesthetics that causes massive calcium release in muscles. Succinylcholine can cause severe hyperkalemia in patients with denervation injuries
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Myalgias are a common postoperative complaint of patients who receive large doses of succinylcholine, possibly the result of muscle fasciculations caused by depolarization. Which drug administered in the operating room can be used to prevent postoperative pain caused by succinylcholine:
explanation: Succinylcholine can cause severe hyperkalemia in patients with denervation injuries due to the widespread depolarization of up-regulated receptors. To reduce the post-operative muscle pain (myalgia) caused by succinylcholine-induced fasciculations, agents like lidocaine or small doses of non-depolarizing blockers may be used to stabilize the muscle membrane.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which drug is most likely to cause hyperkalemia leading to cardiac arrest in patients with spinal cord injuries:
explanation: Dantrolene is the essential emergency treatment for malignant hyperthermia, a life-threatening reaction to certain anesthetics that causes massive calcium release in muscles. Succinylcholine can cause severe hyperkalemia in patients with denervation injuries (like spinal cord trauma) due to the widespread depolarization of up-regulated receptors.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Curare has been given as a paralyzing agent in patients undergoing surgical procedures. Its mode of action is best described by inhibiting the action of which of the following:
explanation: Non-depolarizing blockers, such as tubocurarine and curare, work by competitively binding to nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ). By acting as antagonists at the postsynaptic motor end plate, they prevent acetylcholine from binding and initiating muscle contraction, a process known as a non-depolarizing or stabilizing blockade. These drugs do not "destroy" the junction; they simply block the receptors until the drug is metabolized or out-competed.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Neuromuscular paralysis produced by Rocuronium is blocked by:
explanation: Neostigmine has significant effects on skeletal muscle and can be used to reverse the paralysis produced by non-depolarizing blockers like rocuronium.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Ms Brown, a 28-year-old accountant, has been treated for myasthenia gravis for several years. She reports recent onset of weakness of her hands, diplopia, and difficulty swallowing. Which of the following is the best drug for distinguishing between myasthenic crisis and cholinergic crisis:
explanation: To distinguish between a myasthenic crisis (under-medicated) and a cholinergic crisis (over-medicated), clinicians use the Tensilon test (edrophonium); a brief improvement in symptoms confirms the need for more medication.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Mr Green has just been diagnosed with dysautonomia. Pyridostigmine and neostigmine may cause which one of the following in this patient:
explanation: Neostigmine acts primarily by reversibly inhibiting the enzyme acetylcholinesterase, thereby increasing the amount of acetylcholine available at both muscarinic and nicotinic sites. Common side effects of this increased acetylcholine include diarrhea due to overstimulation of the gastrointestinal tract's muscarinic receptors.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Tetrodotoxin acts on:
explanation: Tetrodotoxin is a potent neurotoxin that acts specifically on the nerve axon by blocking the voltage-gated sodium channels. Unlike neuromuscular blockers that target the junction or end plate, tetrodotoxin prevents the transmission of action potentials along the entire nerve fiber.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Tubocurarine acts on:
explanation: Non-depolarizing blockers, such as tubocurarine and curare, work by competitively binding to nicotinic receptors at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ).
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Neostigmine acts on:
explanation: Neostigmine acts primarily by reversibly inhibiting the enzyme acetylcholinesterase, thereby increasing the amount of acetylcholine available at both muscarinic and nicotinic sites.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which muscle is usually the last to be paralyzed and the first to recover from neuromuscular blockade:
explanation: During neuromuscular blockade, muscles are paralyzed in a specific sequence: small, fast-moving muscles like the hands and feet go first, while the diaphragm is usually the last to be paralyzed and the first to recover.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Bupivacaine is used for surgical procedures as a/an:
explanation: Bupivacaine acts as a local anesthetic, working to block voltage-gated sodium channels, thereby preventing their influx.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Nicotinic receptors mimic the action of:
explanation: Nicotinic receptors are ionotropic receptors that directly open a cation channel upon the binding of acetylcholine, making them distinct from G-protein coupled receptors. They are located at the neuromuscular end plate.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Full activation of Parasympathetic nervous system produces:
explanation: Full activation of the parasympathetic nervous system is often summarized by the "rest and digest" response, which includes pupillary constriction (miosis). This is achieved through the stimulation of muscarinic receptors on the circular sphincter muscle of the iris.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: M2 receptors are located:
explanation: M2 muscarinic receptors are primarily located in the heart, particularly in the SA and AV nodes and the atria. When stimulated by acetylcholine, these G-protein coupled receptors act to slow the heart rate and decrease the force of contraction.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Stimulation of which subtype of the muscarinic cholinergic receptor causes miosis:
explanation: The constriction of the pupil, or miosis, is specifically mediated by the M3 subtype of muscarinic cholinergic receptors. These receptors are located on the pupillary sphincter muscle and work through the Gq-phospholipase C pathway to increase intracellular calcium and cause muscle contraction.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which receptor don’t act through G-protein signaling pathway:
explanation: Nicotinic receptors are ionotropic receptors that directly open a cation channel upon the binding of acetylcholine, making them distinct from G-protein coupled receptors. They are located at the neuromuscular end plate, where they mediate skeletal muscle contraction, and in autonomic ganglia.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: An elderly man is brought to the emergency room after ingesting a large quantity of prazosin tablet (alpha 1 antagonist), which mediates effects of epinephrine & norepinephrine on blood vessels & urinary bladder. Which symptom is most likely to be seen in this patient:
explanation: Prazosin is a selective alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist that blocks the receptors responsible for causing vasoconstriction in peripheral blood vessels. In an overdose, this results in widespread dilation of vessels (vasodilation), which can lead to a severe drop in blood pressure (hypotension). Patients may also experience reflex tachycardia, but the primary vascular effect is the loss of smooth muscle tone in the arteries and veins.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following is a sign of alpha receptor activation:
explanation: Activation of alpha-1 adrenergic receptors on the radial muscle of the iris leads to pupillary dilation (mydriasis). This sympathetic response increases the amount of light entering the eye, which is beneficial during "fight or flight" situations.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Botulinum toxins impair all types of cholinergic transmission. If a child presents with ingestion of botulinum toxin, which sign/symptom will confirm the diagnosis of botulinum poisoning:

options.c: Cycloplegia (loss of accommodating power of eye)

explanation: Botulinum toxin prevents the release of acetylcholine from all cholinergic nerve terminals, leading to a total loss of parasympathetic and motor signaling. In the eye, the loss of acetylcholine prevents the ciliary muscle from contracting, leading to cycloplegia, or the loss of the eye's ability to accommodate for near vision. This paralysis of the internal eye muscles is a classic diagnostic sign of botulinum poisoning.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Beta 2 receptors are present on:
explanation: Beta-2 adrenergic receptors are widely distributed throughout the body, notably on smooth muscle (causing bronchodilation and vasodilation), the liver (promoting glycogenolysis), and the heart. Their activation by adrenaline during stress helps to open the airways and mobilize glucose for energy. While Beta-1 receptors are the dominant type in the heart for increasing rate and force, Beta-2 receptors also play a minor role in cardiac stimulation.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which physiological change is expected when the sympathetic system is inhibited using a pharmacological agent:
explanation: The sympathetic nervous system is responsible for maintaining heart rate and blood pressure through the release of noradrenaline; therefore, inhibiting this system results in a reduction in heart rate. By blocking the stimulatory effects on the SA node and the vasculature, pharmacological inhibitors (like beta-blockers) effectively lower cardiac output and promote vasodilation.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which receptors are present at neuromuscular end plate:
explanation: Nicotinic receptors are ionotropic receptors that directly open a cation channel upon the binding of acetylcholine, making them distinct from G-protein coupled receptors. They are located at the neuromuscular end plate, where they mediate skeletal muscle contraction, and in autonomic ganglia.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following neurotransmitters is released normally in SA node of heart in response to increased heart rate:
explanation: In response to a need for increased cardiac output, the sympathetic nervous system releases nor-adrenaline(norepinephrine) at the SA node of the heart. This neurotransmitter binds to Beta-1 receptors, which increases the rate of pacemaker firing and the force of contraction. Acetylcholine, by contrast, is the parasympathetic neurotransmitter that acts to decrease the heart rate.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs block the effects of adrenaline and noradrenaline on the heart and blood vessels, leading to decreased heart rate and decreased blood pressure:
explanation: Beta-blockers are the correct choice for the first question because they competitively antagonize β-receptors, resulting in a decreased heart rate and reduced blood pressure by blocking sympathetic discharge. Muscarinic antagonists are incorrect because they typically increase heart rate. Alpha blockers are also wrong in this context because, while they reduce vascular tone to lower blood pressure, they often cause a compensatory reflex tachycardia, (Reflex in heart to avoid reduced pressure in vessels) which increases the heart rate as alpha receptors are not present in cardiac muscle. Angiotensin receptor blockers are incorrect as they target the hormonal RAAS pathway rather than directly blocking the immediate effects of adrenaline and noradrenaline on the heart and vessels
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs increase the excretion of sodium and water by the kidneys, leading to decreased blood volume and decreased blood pressure:
explanation: Diuretics are the correct drugs for the second question as they increase the renal excretion of sodium and water, which reduces blood volume and effectively lowers blood pressure. Beta-blockers and vasodilators are incorrect because they lower blood pressure by modifying heart rate or vascular resistance rather than through direct fluid excretion mechanisms. Calcium channel blockers are also wrong because they primarily act on vascular smooth muscle to cause dilation and do not have a primary mechanism of action involving renal sodium excretion.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: A man is admitted to the emergency department with a brownish cyanotic appearance, marked shortness of breath, and hypotension. Which of the following is most likely to cause methemoglobinemia:
explanation: Amyl nitrite, because it is a highly volatile, rapid-acting nitrate that can induce methemoglobinemia, leading to a brownish cyanotic appearance and shortness of breath. Isosorbide dinitrate and isosorbide mononitrate are incorrect because, while they are nitrates used for angina, they are less commonly associated with acute, severe methemoglobinemia in an emergency setting compared to amyl nitrite. Sodium cyanide is incorrect as it is a metabolic toxin that inhibits cellular respiration rather than primarily causing methemoglobinemia, although nitrites are actually used as part of the treatment for cyanide poisoning.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs block the production of angiotensin II, a hormone that causes blood vessels to constrict, leading to decreased blood pressure:
explanation: ACE specifically block the angiotensin-converting enzyme, preventing the production of Angiotensin II, a potent vasoconstrictor. Alpha-blockers are incorrect because they block adrenoceptors on blood vessels and do not interfere with hormone production. Renin inhibitors are wrong because they block the system earlier (the conversion of angiotensinogen to Angiotensin I), and aldosterone antagonists are incorrect as they block the downstream effects of aldosterone rather than the production of Angiotensin II.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs increase the amount of calcium that enters the heart muscle cells, increasing the strength of contractions and slowing the heart rate:
explanation: Digitalis glycosides are correct because they increase the availability of calcium within heart cells, which enhances the strength of contraction (positive inotropy) while slowing the heart rate through vagal effects. Calcium channel blockers are incorrect because they reduce the entry of calcium into cardiac cells, which would decrease contraction strength. Beta-blockers are also wrong because they generally decrease both heart rate and the strength of contraction by inhibiting sympathetic stimulation
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs is used to dilate blood vessels and improve blood flow in peripheral vascular diseases:
explanation: Nitric oxide donors, such as certain specialized nitrates or drugs like nebivolol, are the correct answer for the sixth question because they dilate blood vessels to improve flow in peripheral vascular diseases. Beta-blockers are typically incorrect for this purpose because beta2 blockade can sometimes lead to peripheral vasoconstriction (β₂ blockade = ↓ vasodilation → relatively ↑ vasoconstriction), potentially worsening the disease. ACE inhibitors and calcium channel blockers are used for hypertension but are not categorized primarily for vasodilation as "nitric oxide donors" in the context of vascular relaxation mechanisms
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs block the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) and reduce the workload on the heart in heart failure:
explanation: ACE inhibitors are the correct choice for the seventh question as they directly block the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) by preventing the formation of Angiotensin II, thereby reducing the workload on the heart. Beta-blockers are also used in heart failure to reduce workload and mortality, but they do so by blocking sympathetic activation rather than the RAAS itself. Diuretics and cardiac glycosides are incorrect as they manage fluid symptoms or increase contractility, respectively, without acting as primary RAAS blockers
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs increase blood flow to the heart muscle by dilating blood vessels, leading to decreased oxygen demand and decreased chest pain:
explanation: Nitrates are the correct because they act as potent vasodilators that dilate veins and coronary arteries, increasing blood flow to the heart while reducing the oxygen demand. Beta-blockers are incorrect because, while they do decrease oxygen demand and chest pain, they do so by slowing the heart rate rather than by dilating vessels to increase flow. Ranolazine is wrong because it works by modifying specific cardiac ion currents rather than being a primary vasodilator.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs has been shown to prolong life in patients with chronic congestive failure in spite of having a negative inotropic effect on cardiac contractility:
explanation: Beta-blockers are the correct choice for the question because they have been shown in large clinical trials to reduce morbidity and mortality in patients with chronic heart failure. While they possess a negative inotropic effect (decreasing the force of contraction), their use in Chronic heart failure helps to reduce pathological hypertrophy of heart. Their benefit comes from blocking chronic sympathetic overactivation, reducing arrhythmias, preventing adverse remodeling, and improving ventricular function over time. Digoxin is incorrect because it is a positive inotrope that increases contraction force but does not necessarily prolong life. ACE inhibitors (e.g., Enalapril) DO reduce mortality in heart failure but are not negative inotropes, which is why they are not the correct answer here.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following antihypertensive drug acts by releasing nitric oxide:
explanation: Nitroprusside is the intended answer for the question, as it is a well-known antihypertensive that acts through the release of nitric oxide. Atenolol is a β 1 -selective blocker that lacks this nitric oxide mechanism. Captopril is an ACE inhibitor, and Losartan is an ARB; both lower blood pressure by interfering with the renin-angiotensin system rather than by direct nitric oxide release
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which one of the following drugs is associated with clinically useful or physiologically important positive inotropic effect:
explanation: Digoxin (a cardiac glycoside) is the correct answer for the third question because it is specifically associated with a physiologically important positive inotropic effect, meaning it increases the strength of cardiac muscle contraction. Beta-blockers are wrong because they are negative inotropes. ACE inhibitors and diuretics are incorrect as they reduce the heart's workload by managing fluid volume or vascular resistance rather than directly increasing the force of the heart muscle's contraction.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs reduce the amount of work the heart has to do by decreasing the volume of blood it pumps, leading to decreased oxygen demand:
explanation: Nitrates are the correct choice for the fourth question because they vasodilate veins, which reduces the volume of blood returning to the heart (preload). This reduction in volume leads to decreased oxygen demand and reduced cardiac work. Beta-blockers also decrease oxygen demand, but they do so primarily by slowing the heart rate rather than by reducing blood volume. Digitalis glycosides are incorrect because they increase the force of contraction, which can actually increase oxygen demand. Alpha agonists are wrong because they cause vasoconstriction, which increases the heart's workload
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which one of the following is characteristic of nifedipine treatment in patients with essential hypertension:

options.a: Competitively blocks angiotensin II at its receptor
options.b: Decreases calcium efflux from skeletal muscle
options.c: Decreases renin concentration in the blood
options.d: Decreases calcium influx into smooth muscle

explanation: Decreasing calcium influx into smooth muscle is the correct characteristic of nifedipine for the question. Nifedipine belongs to the dihydropyridine class of calcium channel blockers, which interact with L-type voltage-gated channels to prevent calcium from entering smooth vascular muscle cells, leading to vasodilation and lower blood pressure. It does not block Angiotensin II, which is the role of ARBs. It does not significantly affect skeletal muscle calcium efflux or primarily decrease blood renin levels.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: There are different classes of drugs used in treatment of hypertension. Which class of drug act by vasodilation:
explanation: Calcium blockers are the correct as they are a major class of drugs that act through vasodilation to treat hypertension. Diuretics are incorrect because they lower blood pressure by reducing blood volume through salt and water excretion. ACE inhibitors also lead to vasodilation indirectly by preventing the formation of Angiotensin II, but "Calcium blocker" is the most direct answer for a drug class defined by its vasodilatory action on the vessels. Antihistamines are incorrect as they are not standard antihypertensive medications
subject: Pharmacology

statement: The cellular action of calcium channel blockers is:

options.a: They inhibit Na + /Ca 2+ exchanger in cardiac and smooth muscles
options.b: They interact with the L-type voltage-gated channel
options.c: Their interaction with the sodium pump results in the inhibition of calcium transport
options.d: Their interaction with the membrane phospholipids results in a nonselective decrease in ion transport

explanation: The correct cellular action for calcium channel blockers in the seventh question is that they interact with the L-type voltage-gated channel. By binding to these channels, they reduce the inward calcium current during the action potential, which relaxes the muscle and slows conduction in sensitive tissues like the AV node. They do not primarily act on the Na+/Ca2+ exchanger, the sodium pump, or membrane phospholipids.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs increase the force of heart contractions and improve cardiac output in heart failure:
explanation: Cardiac glycosides (such as digoxin) are the correct choice for the eighth question because they increase the force of heart contractions (positive inotropy), which helps improve cardiac output in heart failure. Beta-blockers are incorrect in this specific context because they decrease the force of contraction. ACE inhibitors and diuretics are used in heart failure but work by reducing the resistance the heart must pump against or reduce fluid volume, rather than by directly increasing the strength of the heart muscle itself
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs block the flow of sodium into the heart muscle cells, reducing the rate of depolarization and preventing arrhythmias:
explanation: Class I antiarrhythmic drugs are the correct answer for the ninth question because their primary mechanism is to block the flow of sodium into heart muscle cells during Phase 0 of the action potential. This action reduces the rate of depolarization and slows conduction velocity to prevent arrhythmias. Beta-blockers (Class II) work by blocking sympathetic effects and slowing the pacemaker activity. Digitalis glycosides are not classified as primary sodium channel blockers for arrhythmias
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs block the effects of adrenaline and noradrenaline on blood vessels, leading to relaxation of blood vessels and decreased blood pressure:
explanation: Alpha-blockers are the correct answer for the final question because they specifically block the adrenoceptors on blood vessels that respond to adrenaline and noradrenaline. By inhibiting these receptors, the drugs cause a reduction in vascular tone, leading to the relaxation of blood vessels and a decrease in blood pressure. Diuretics and ARBs lower blood pressure through different hormonal or fluid-based pathways. Calcium channel blockers cause relaxation by blocking ion channels rather than adrenoceptors
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following is the best-documented mechanism of beneficial action of cardiac glycosides:

options.a: A decrease in calcium uptake by SR
options.c: A modification of the actin molecule
options.d: An increase in systolic cytoplasmic calcium levels

explanation: an increase in systolic cytoplasmic calcium levels is the correct mechanism because cardiac glycosides (like digoxin) increase the availability of calcium in the sarcoplasm. This calcium interacts with troponin to initiate cross-bridge formation and strengthen the contraction. A decrease in calcium uptake by the SR is wrong because the drug typically enhances stored calcium levels for release. An increase in ATP synthesis is incorrect as these drugs inhibit an ATPase rather than synthesizing it. Modification of the actin molecule is wrong because the drug affects concentrations, not the protein structure of actin itself.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which group of drug has no role in arrhythmia:
explanation: Chloride channel blockers have no role in arrhythmia management according to the standard classification in the sources. The four major classes of antiarrhythmics are Sodium channel blockers (Class I), Beta-blockers (Class II), Potassium channel blockers (Class III), and Calcium channel blockers (Class IV). Chloride channels are not involved in the standard therapeutic suppression of abnormal pacemakers or conduction
subject: Pharmacology

statement: The cellular mechanism of action of digoxin is:
explanation: The cellular mechanism of digoxin is the inhibition of Na+/K+ ATPase. The sources indicate that sodium-calcium exchange depends on the sodium-potassium ATPase pump to maintain gradients. Inhibiting this pump indirectly increases intracellular calcium, leading to increased contractility. Inhibition of cAMP synthesis is wrong as that is associated with beta-blocker mechanisms. Inhibition of b-adrenergic stimulation is also the role of beta-blockers, not digoxin. Inhibition of ATP degradation is incorrect because digoxin targets the pump protein specifically
subject: Pharmacology

statement: A pregnant lady presents with hypertension. Which anti-hypertensive drug should not be given to that lady:
explanation: ACE inhibitors (ACEi) should not be given to a pregnant lady because they interfere with the renin-angiotensin system, which is essential for fetal development. While Hydralazine, Nifedipine, and certain Beta blockers are often used to manage hypertension in pregnancy, ACE inhibitors and ARBs are clinically documented as contraindicated due to fetal risks
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Dobutamine (beta-1 agonist) increases cardiac contractility by:
explanation: Dobutamine increases cardiac contractility by the activation of adenylyl cyclase. As a beta1 agonist, it stimulates the production of cAMP; this is the opposite of beta-blockers, which reduce cAMP. Inactivation of Na channels is wrong because that is the mechanism of Class I antiarrhythmics. Inhibition of Na+/K+ ATPase is wrong because that is the mechanism of digoxin. Activation of Na/Cl cotransporters is related to diuretic action, not cardiac contractility.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Main side effect of digoxin:
explanation: The main side effect of digoxin is nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. The sources note that cardiac glycosides are frequently associated with gastrointestinal upsetting. Heart failure is wrong because digoxin is actually used as a treatment for it. Liver failure and hypertension are not primary toxicities of this drug class
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Main side effect of beta blocker (propranolol):
explanation: The main side effect of the nonselective beta blocker (propranolol) is that it aggravates asthma. The beta 2 receptors are vital for preventing bronchospasm, and nonselective blockers can trigger severe asthma attacks by inhibiting these receptors. Headache is less common than bronchospasm, and these drugs are used to treat hypertension, not cause it. Orthostatic hypotension is more characteristic of alpha-blockers than beta-blockers
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Losartan belongs to which antihypertensive group:
explanation: Losartan belongs to the ARBs (Angiotensin Receptor Blockers) group. It lowers blood pressure by blocking the receptors for Angiotensin II. It is not a Beta blocker, Calcium channel blocker, or Diuretic, as those belong to distinct drug classes with different mechanisms of action such as heart rate reduction or fluid excretion.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Beta blockers are used in treatment of angina because of all reasons except:
explanation: Beta blockers are used in angina because they reduce the double product (heart rate times blood pressure) and decrease the overall myocardial oxygen requirement by slowing the heart rate and reducing contractility. However, they are the "except" choice for reducing vasospasm because in vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina, beta blockers can actually worsen the condition by leaving alpha-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed. Reducing cardiac size is a beneficial effect of reducing the workload and wall stress
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Class I antiarrhythmic drug:
explanation: Procainamide and Flecainide are both correct for the second question as they are Class I antiarrhythmic drugs; Procainamide belongs to Subclass 1A and Flecainide belongs to 1C, both acting by blocking sodium channels. Adenosine is incorrect as it is a miscellaneous agent that primarily acts on the AV node by hyperpolarizing tissue through potassium currents
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drugs is not used for the management of congestive heart failure:
explanation: Calcium channel blockers (specifically non-dihydropyridines like verapamil) are generally not used for the primary management of congestive heart failure because their negative inotropic effects can further depress a failing heart. In contrast, ACE inhibitors, Beta blockers (like metoprolol or carvedilol), and Digoxin are all standard therapies used to reduce mortality or manage symptoms in heart failure patients
subject: Pharmacology

statement: The antiarrhythmic drug that primarily belongs to Ia Na channel group:
explanation: Quinidine is the correct answer for the fourth question because it is a prototype Group 1A antiarrhythmic that blocks sodium channels and prolongs the action potential. Amiodarone belongs to Group 3, Adenosine is a miscellaneous agent, and Acebutalol is a Group 2 beta-blocker
subject: Pharmacology

statement: ACEi/ARBs reduces the:
explanation: ACE inhibitors and ARBs reduce both preload and afterload; they reduce afterload by preventing the production or action of Angiotensin II, a potent vasoconstrictor, and they reduce preload by inhibiting aldosterone secretion, which decreases sodium and water retention. They do not primarily increase cardiac output directly, though it may improve as a secondary effect of reduced resistance
subject: Pharmacology

statement: The therapeutic benefit of propranolol (beta blocker) in angina is believed to be primarily due to:

options.b: A decreased requirement of myocardial oxygen

explanation: the primary therapeutic benefit of propranolol in angina is a decreased requirement of myocardial oxygen. By blocking beta1 receptors, it reduces heart rate and the force of contraction, which are the main determinants of oxygen demand during exertion. It does not reduce the production of catecholamines (A) or increase peripheral resistance (D) as its primary goal in angina therapy.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: All of the followings are ways of treating angina except:
explanation: Treating angina involves reducing oxygen requirement (via beta-blockers or nitrates), increasing oxygen delivery (via vasodilators), and increasing the efficiency of oxygen utilization. An increase in end-diastolic volume is the "except" choice because higher volume increases ventricular wall tension, which actually increases oxygen demand and worsens ischemia
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following drug is calcium channel blocker used in treatment of hypertension:
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which drug group causes hyperkalemia:
explanation: ACE inhibitors and ARBs are the drug groups that cause hyperkalemia. By blocking the effects of aldosterone, these drugs prevent the normal exchange of sodium for potassium in the distal tubule, leading to potassium retention in the blood. Diuretics (unless potassium-sparing) usually cause hypokalemia, and alpha/beta blockers do not typically have hyperkalemia as a primary side effect.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which antihypertensive drug causes cough & angioedema:
explanation: ACE inhibitors (ACEi) are uniquely associated with the side effects of cough and angioedema; the cough is generally attributed to the accumulation of bradykinin in the lungs when its degradative enzyme is inhibited. ARBs are often used as an alternative because they do not affect bradykinin levels and thus rarely cause a cough.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: In a patient suffering from angina of effort, nitroglycerin may be given sublingually because of this mode of administration:

options.c: Has a decreased tendency to cause methemoglobinemia

explanation: Nitroglycerin is given sublingually in effort angina because this route allows the drug to be absorbed directly into the systemic circulation through the mucous membranes, thereby avoiding first-pass hepatic metabolism. This ensures a rapid onset of action and higher bioavailability compared to the oral route, where the liver would rapidly degrade the drug.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which should not be given in vasospastic angina:
explanation: Beta blockers should not be given in vasospastic (Prinzmetal) angina because they can exacerbate coronary vasospasm by leaving alpha-adrenoceptor-mediated vasoconstriction unopposed. Nitroglycerin and calcium channel blockers are the preferred treatments as they directly relax the coronary vascular smooth muscle to relieve the spasm.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: A 40-year-old male presents in emergency with left-sided chest pain radiating towards left arm and sweating. ECG shows ST elevation. What should be given to this patient:
explanation: A 40-year-old male with chest pain and ST elevation on ECG is experiencing a transmural myocardial infarction. He should be given Nitroglycerin and Aspirin immediately; aspirin limits further thrombosis by inhibiting platelet aggregation, while nitroglycerin vasodilates veins and coronary arteries to minimize ischemia
subject: Pharmacology

statement: The antiarrhythmic drug that primarily belongs to group 3 (potassium channel blocker):
explanation: Amiodarone is the correct answer for the final question as it is the most efficacious Group 3 antiarrhythmic drug, acting primarily as a potassium channel blocker that prolongs the action potential duration and the effective refractory period. Quinidine is Group 1A, Adenosine is miscellaneous, and Acebutalol is Group 2.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: A 12-year-old girl, known patient of asthma, presents to the emergency room with complaints of cough, dyspnea & wheezing after visiting a riding stable. Which is the most appropriate drug to rapidly reverse her bronchoconstriction:
explanation: A 12-year-old girl with asthma experiencing acute bronchoconstriction after allergen exposure should be treated with inhaled albuterol, a short-acting β2 agonist, because it rapidly relaxes bronchial smooth muscle. Inhaled corticosteroids like fluticasone or beclomethasone act slowly and are preventive, not for immediate relief, while IV propranolol is a β-blocker and would worsen bronchospasm.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Theophylline is believed to benefit asthma patients by exerting the following actions except:
explanation: Theophylline benefits asthma by causing bronchodilation, improving diaphragmatic contractility, and reducing mediator release, but it does not inhibit antigen-antibody reactions—this is not its mechanism.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following is an inhibitor of leukotriene receptors:
explanation: Montelukast inhibits leukotriene receptors, preventing bronchoconstriction and inflammation. Albuterol (beta agonist), aminophylline, and ipratropium do not act on leukotriene pathways.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Which of the following pharmacologic agents antagonizes adenosine:
explanation: Theophyline works by blocking adenosine receptors in bronchial smooth muscle. Adenosine normally promotes bronchoconstriction and release of inflammatory mediators, so when theophylline blocks these receptors, it leads to bronchodilation and improved airflow. Terbutaline is a β2 agonist that causes bronchodilation by increasing cAMP, not by blocking adenosine. Glucocorticoids reduce inflammation by altering gene expression; they do not act on adenosine receptors. Cromolyn sodium stabilizes mast cells and prevents mediator release but does not block adenosine.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: The following antiasthma drug is not a bronchodilator:
explanation: Glucocorticoids reduce inflammation, hyperreactivity, and are prophylactic, but they are not bronchodilators—they do not relax smooth muscle directly. Ipratropium bromide is antimuscurinic drug, Formoterol is a beta agonist and Theophyline all cause bronchodilation.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Because of its anti-inflammatory mechanism of action, which drug requires regular administration for treatment of asthma:
explanation: Inhaled fluticasone is correct because it is a corticosteroid with anti-inflammatory action that requires regular use to control asthma. It does not act immediately. In contrast, inhaled albuterol and ipratropium are bronchodilators used for quick relief, not long-term inflammation control, and oral zafirlukast is also preventive but the question emphasizes the classic inhaled anti-inflammatory mainstay.
subject: Pharmacology

statement: Cromolyn is useful in many patients with asthma because it inhibits:
